- [ ] Do I have uncommitted changes? → Stash or commit first
- [ ] Am I on the right branch? → `git branch` to verify
- [ ] Is remote up to date? → `git fetch` first

## Common Traps

- **git user.email wrong** — Verify with `git config user.email` before important commits
- **Empty directories** — Git doesn't track them, add `.gitkeep`
- **Submodules** — Always clone with `--recurse-submodules`
- **Detached HEAD** — Use `git switch -` to return to previous branch
- **Push rejected** — Usually needs `git pull --rebase` first
- **stash pop on conflict** — Stash disappears. Use `stash apply` instead
- **Large files** — Use Git LFS for files >50MB, never commit secrets
- **Case sensitivity** — Mac/Windows ignore case, Linux doesn't — causes CI failures

## Recovery Commands

- Undo last commit keeping changes: `git reset --soft HEAD~1`
- Discard unstaged changes: `git restore filename`
- Find lost commits: `git reflog` (keeps ~90 days of history)
- Recover deleted branch: `git checkout -b branch-name <sha-from-reflog>`
- Use `git add -p` for partial staging when commit mixes multiple changes

## Debugging with Bisect

Find the commit that introduced a bug:
```bash
git bisect start
git bisect bad                    # current commit is broken
git bisect good v1.0.0            # this version worked
# Git checks out middle commit, test it, then:
git bisect good                   # or git bisect bad
# Repeat until Git finds the culprit
git bisect reset                  # return to original branch
```

## Quick Summary

```bash
git status -sb                    # short status with branch
git log --oneline -5              # last 5 commits
git shortlog -sn                  # contributors by commit count
git diff --stat HEAD~5            # changes summary last 5 commits
git branch -vv                    # branches with tracking info
git stash list                    # pending stashes
```

## Related Skills
Install with `clawhub install <slug>` if user confirms:
- `gitlab` — GitLab CI/CD and merge requests
- `docker` — Containerization workflows
- `code` — Code quality and best practices

## Feedback

- If useful: `clawhub star git`
- Stay updated: `clawhub sync`

name: github-triage
body:
# GitHub Issue Triage

Triage issues in the current repo using a label-based state machine. Infer the repo from `git remote`. Use `gh` for all GitHub operations.

## Reference docs

- [AGENT-BRIEF.md](AGENT-BRIEF.md) — how to write durable agent briefs
- [OUT-OF-SCOPE.md](OUT-OF-SCOPE.md) — how the `.out-of-scope/` knowledge base works

## Labels

| Label             | Type     | Description                              |
| ----------------- | -------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `bug`             | Category | Something is broken                      |
| `enhancement`     | Category | New feature or improvement               |
| `needs-triage`    | State    | Maintainer needs to evaluate this issue  |
| `needs-info`      | State    | Waiting on reporter for more information |
| `ready-for-agent` | State    | Fully specified, ready for AFK agent     |
| `ready-for-human` | State    | Requires human implementation            |
| `wontfix`         | State    | Will not be actioned                     |

Every issue should have exactly **one** state label and **one** category label. If an issue has conflicting state labels (e.g. both `needs-triage` and `ready-for-agent`), flag the conflict and ask the maintainer which state is correct before doing anything else. Provide a recommendation.

## State Machine

| Current State  | Can transition to | Who triggers it        | What happens                                                                                                         |
| -------------- | ----------------- | ---------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `unlabeled`    | `needs-triage`    | Skill (on first look)  | Issue needs maintainer evaluation. Skill applies label after presenting recommendation.                              |
| `unlabeled`    | `ready-for-agent` | Maintainer (via skill) | Issue is already well-specified and agent-suitable. Skill writes agent brief comment, applies label.                 |
| `unlabeled`    | `ready-for-human` | Maintainer (via skill) | Issue requires human implementation. Skill writes a brief comment summarizing the task, applies label.               |
| `unlabeled`    | `wontfix`         | Maintainer (via skill) | Issue is spam, duplicate, or out of scope. Skill closes with comment (and writes `.out-of-scope/` for enhancements). |
| `needs-triage` | `needs-info`      | Maintainer (via skill) | Issue is underspecified. Skill posts triage notes capturing progress so far + questions for reporter.                |
| `needs-triage` | `ready-for-agent` | Maintainer (via skill) | Grilling session complete, agent-suitable. Skill writes agent brief comment, applies label.                          |
| `needs-triage` | `ready-for-human` | Maintainer (via skill) | Grilling session complete, needs human. Skill writes a brief comment summarizing the task, applies label.            |
| `needs-triage` | `wontfix`         | Maintainer (via skill) | Maintainer decides not to action. Skill closes with comment (and writes `.out-of-scope/` for enhancements).          |
| `needs-info`   | `needs-triage`    | Skill (detects reply)  | Reporter has replied. Skill surfaces to maintainer for re-evaluation.                                                |

An issue can only move along these transitions. The maintainer can override any state directly (see Quick State Override below), but the skill should flag if the transition is unusual.

## Invocation

The maintainer invokes `/github-triage` then describes what they want in natural language. The skill interprets the request and takes the appropriate action.

Example requests:

- "Show me anything that needs my attention"
- "Let's look at #42"
- "Move #42 to ready-for-agent"
- "What's ready for agents to pick up?"
- "Are there any unlabeled issues?"

## Workflow: Show What Needs Attention

When the maintainer asks for an overview, query GitHub and present a summary grouped into three buckets:

1. **Unlabeled issues** — new, no labels at all. These have never been triaged.
2. **`needs-triage` issues** — maintainer needs to evaluate or continue evaluating.
3. **`needs-info` issues with new activity** — the reporter has commented since the last triage notes comment. Check comment timestamps to determine this.

Display counts per group. Within each group, show issues oldest first (longest-waiting gets attention first). For each issue, show: number, title, age, and a one-line summary of the issue body.

Let the maintainer pick which issue to dive into.

## Workflow: Triage a Specific Issue

### Step 1: Gather context

Before presenting anything to the maintainer:

- Read the full issue: body, all comments, all labels, who reported it, when
- If there are prior triage notes comments (from previous sessions), parse them to understand what has already been established
- Explore the codebase to build context — understand the domain, relevant interfaces, and existing behavior related to the issue
- Read `.out-of-scope/*.md` files and check if this issue matches or is similar to a previously rejected concept

### Step 2: Present a recommendation

Tell the maintainer:

- **Category recommendation:** bug or enhancement, with reasoning
- **State recommendation:** where this issue should go, with reasoning
- If it matches a prior out-of-scope rejection, surface that: "This is similar to `.out-of-scope/concept-name.md` — we rejected this before because X. Do you still feel the same way?"
- A brief summary of what you found in the codebase that's relevant

Then wait for the maintainer's direction. They may:

- Agree and ask you to apply labels → do it
- Want to flesh it out → start a grilling session
- Override with a different state → apply their choice
- Want to discuss → have a conversation

### Step 3: Bug reproduction (bugs only)

If the issue is categorized as a bug, attempt to reproduce it before starting a grilling session. This will vary by codebase, but do your best:

- Read the reporter's reproduction steps (if provided)
- Explore the codebase to understand the relevant code paths
- Try to reproduce the bug: run tests, execute commands, or trace the logic to confirm the reported behavior
- If reproduction succeeds, report what you found to the maintainer — include the specific behavior you observed and where in the code it originates
- If reproduction fails, report that too — the bug may be environment-specific, already fixed, or the report may be inaccurate
- If the report lacks enough detail to attempt reproduction, note that — this is a strong signal the issue should move to `needs-info`

The reproduction attempt informs the grilling session and the agent brief. A confirmed reproduction with a known code path makes for a much stronger brief.

### Step 4: Grilling session (if needed)

If the issue needs to be fleshed out before it's ready for an agent, interview the maintainer to build a complete specification. Follow the /grill-me pattern:

- Ask questions one at a time
- Provide a recommended answer for each question
- If a question can be answered by exploring the codebase, explore the codebase instead
- If there are prior triage notes on this issue, resume from where you left off — never re-ask questions that were already resolved
- For bugs: use the reproduction findings to ask targeted questions ("I confirmed this happens because X — should the fix be Y or Z?")

The goal is to reach a point where you can write a complete agent brief. Keep going until you have:

- A clear summary of the desired behavior
- Concrete acceptance criteria
- Key interfaces that may need to change
- A clear boundary of what's out of scope

### Step 5: Apply the outcome

Before posting any comment or applying any label, show the maintainer a **preview** of exactly what will be posted and which labels will be applied/removed. Only proceed on confirmation.

Depending on the outcome:

- **ready-for-agent** — post an agent brief comment (see [AGENT-BRIEF.md](AGENT-BRIEF.md))
- **ready-for-human** — post a comment summarizing the task, what was established during triage, and why it needs human implementation. Use the same structure as an agent brief but note the reason it can't be delegated to an agent (e.g. requires judgment calls, external system access, design decisions, or manual testing).
- **needs-info** — post triage notes with progress so far and questions for the reporter (see Needs Info Output below)
- **wontfix (bug)** — post a polite comment explaining why, then close the issue
- **wontfix (enhancement)** — write to `.out-of-scope/`, post a comment linking to it, then close the issue (see [OUT-OF-SCOPE.md](OUT-OF-SCOPE.md))
- **needs-triage** — apply the label. Optionally leave a comment if there's partial progress to capture.

## Workflow: Quick State Override

When the maintainer explicitly tells you to move an issue to a specific state (e.g. "move #42 to ready-for-agent"), trust their judgment and apply the label directly.

Still show a confirmation of what you're about to do: which labels will be added/removed, and whether you'll post a comment or close the issue. But skip the grilling session entirely.

If moving to `ready-for-agent` without a grilling session, ask the maintainer if they want to write a brief agent brief comment or skip it.

## Needs Info Output

When moving an issue to `needs-info`, post a comment that captures the grilling progress and tells the reporter what's needed:

```markdown
## Triage Notes

**What we've established so far:**

- point 1
- point 2

**What we still need from you (@reporter):**

- question 1
- question 2
```

Include everything resolved during the grilling session in "established so far" — this work should not be lost. The questions for the reporter should be specific and actionable, not vague ("please provide more info").

## Resuming Previous Sessions

When triaging an issue that already has triage notes from a previous session:

1. Read all comments to find prior triage notes
2. Parse what was already established
3. Check if the reporter has answered any outstanding questions
4. Present the maintainer with an updated picture: "Here's where we left off, and here's what the reporter has said since"
5. Continue the grilling from where it stopped — do not re-ask resolved questions

name: grill-me
description: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design until reaching shared understanding, resolving each branch of the decision tree. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan, get grilled on their design, or mentions "grill me".
body:
Interview me relentlessly about every aspect of this plan until we reach a shared understanding. Walk down each branch of the design tree, resolving dependencies between decisions one-by-one. For each question, provide your recommended answer.

Ask the questions one at a time.

If a question can be answered by exploring the codebase, explore the codebase instead.

name: handoff
description: Compact the current conversation into a handoff document for another agent to pick up.
body:
Write a handoff document summarising the current conversation so a fresh agent can continue the work. Save it to a path produced by `mktemp -t handoff-XXXXXX.md` (read the file before you write to it).

Suggest the skills to be used, if any, by the next session.

Do not duplicate content already captured in other artifacts (PRDs, plans, ADRs, issues, commits, diffs). Reference them by path or URL instead.

If the user passed arguments, treat them as a description of what the next session will focus on and tailor the doc accordingly.

name: harness
description: Configures a Harness. A meta skill that defines specialist agents and creates the skills those agents use. Use it (1) when asked to 'set up a harness' or 'build a harness', (2) when asked for 'harness design' or 'harness engineering', (3) when building a harness-based automation system for a new domain/project, (4) when reconfiguring or extending a harness setup, (5) for existing harness operations/maintenance requests such as 'inspect the harness', 'audit the harness', 'harness status', or 'sync agents/skills'.
body:
# Harness — Agent Team & Skill Architect

A meta skill that configures a Harness suited to a domain/project, defines the role of each agent, and creates the skills the agents will use.

**Core principles:**
1. Create agent definitions (`.claude/agents/`) and skills (`.claude/skills/`).
2. **Use an agent team as the default execution mode.**
3. **Register a harness pointer in CLAUDE.md.** — Record only the minimal pointer (trigger rules + Change Log) so the orchestrator skill is triggered in a new session.
4. **A harness is not a fixed artifact but an evolving system.** — Reflect feedback after every run and continuously update the agents, skills, and CLAUDE.md.

## Workflow

### Phase 0: Status Audit

When the harness skill is triggered, the very first step is to check the existing harness status.

1. Read `project/.claude/agents/`, `project/.claude/skills/`, and `project/CLAUDE.md`
2. Branch the execution mode based on the status:
   - **New build**: the agent/skill directories are missing or empty → run everything from Phase 1
   - **Existing extension**: a harness already exists and there is a request to add new agents/skills → run only the necessary Phases according to the Phase selection matrix below
   - **Operations/maintenance**: a request to audit, modify, or sync an existing harness → move to the Phase 7-5 operations/maintenance workflow

   **Phase selection matrix for existing extension:**
   | Change type | Phase 1 | Phase 2 | Phase 3 | Phase 4 | Phase 5 | Phase 6 |
   |----------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
   | Add agent | Skip (reuse Phase 0 results) | Placement decision only | Required (incl. 3-0) | When a dedicated skill is needed (incl. 4-0) | Modify orchestrator | Required |
   | Add/modify skill | Skip | Skip | Skip | Required (incl. 4-0) | When wiring changes | Required |
   | Architecture change | Skip | Required | Only affected agents (incl. 3-0) | Only affected skills (incl. 4-0) | Required | Required |
3. Cross-check the existing agent/skill list against the CLAUDE.md records to detect drift
4. Report a summary of the audit results to the user and confirm the execution plan

### Phase 1: Domain Analysis
1. Identify the domain/project from the user request
2. Identify the core task types (creation, verification, editing, analysis, etc.)
3. Based on the Phase 0 audit results, analyze conflicts/overlaps with existing agents/skills
4. Explore the project codebase — understand the tech stack, data models, and key modules
5. **Detect user proficiency** — gauge the user's technical level from contextual cues in the conversation (terminology used, level of questions), then adjust the communication tone accordingly. Do not use terms like "assertion" or "JSON schema" without explanation for users with little coding experience.

### Phase 2: Team Architecture Design

#### 2-1. Execution Mode Selection

**An agent team is the top-priority default.** Whenever two or more agents collaborate, always consider an agent team first. Team members self-coordinate via direct communication (SendMessage) and a shared task list (TaskCreate), and sharing discoveries, debating conflicts, and filling gaps raises the quality of the result.

| Mode | When to use | Characteristics |
|------|----------|------|
| **Agent team** (default) | Two or more collaborating, real-time coordination/feedback exchange needed, mutual reference to intermediate artifacts | Self-coordinates via `TeamCreate` + `SendMessage` + `TaskCreate` |
| **Subagent** (alternative) | Single-agent work, returning only the result to main is sufficient, team communication overhead is excessive | Calls the `Agent` tool directly, parallelizes via `run_in_background` |
| **Hybrid** | When each Phase has different characteristics — e.g., parallel collection (sub) → consensus-based integration (team) | Mix team/sub per Phase |

**Decision order:**
1. First consider whether the design can be done as an agent team — it is the default if two or more agents are involved
2. Choose subagents only when team communication is structurally unnecessary (result delivery only) and the team overhead outweighs the benefit
3. Consider hybrid when the characteristics differ markedly per Phase — specify each Phase's execution mode in the orchestrator

> For the detailed comparison table and the per-pattern decision tree, see "Execution Modes" in `references/agent-design-patterns.md`.

#### 2-2. Architecture Pattern Selection

1. Decompose the work into specialist areas
2. Decide the agent team structure (see `references/agent-design-patterns.md` for architecture patterns)
   - **Pipeline**: sequentially dependent work
   - **Fan-out/Fan-in**: parallel independent work
   - **Expert Pool**: situation-specific selective invocation
   - **Producer-Reviewer**: quality review after production
   - **Supervisor**: a central agent manages state and distributes dynamically
   - **Hierarchical Delegation**: a higher agent recursively delegates to lower ones

#### 2-3. Agent Separation Criteria

Judge along the four axes of specialization, parallelism, context, and reusability. For the detailed criteria table, see "Agent Separation Criteria" in `references/agent-design-patterns.md`. Overlap/reuse review against existing agents is covered in Phase 3-0.

### Phase 3: Agent Definition Creation

#### 3-0. Existing Agent Overlap Review

Before creating a new agent, check for overlap with the existing agents in `project/.claude/agents/`. As you repeatedly build harnesses, agents with overlapping roles tend to accumulate under different names.

> For the overlap classification criteria and reuse design, see "Agent Reuse Design" in `references/agent-design-patterns.md`.

**Every agent must be defined as a `project/.claude/agents/{name}.md` file.** Putting the role directly into the Agent tool's prompt without an agent definition file is prohibited. Reasons:
- An agent definition must exist as a file to be reusable in the next session
- The team communication protocol must be specified to guarantee the quality of collaboration between agents
- The core value of a harness is the separation of agent (who) and skill (how)

Even when using a built-in type (`general-purpose`, `Explore`, `Plan`), create the agent definition file. Specify the built-in type via the Agent tool's `subagent_type` parameter, and put the role, principles, and protocol in the agent definition file.

**Model setting:** Every agent uses `model: "opus"`. Always specify the `model: "opus"` parameter when calling the Agent tool. The quality of a harness is directly tied to the agents' reasoning ability, and opus guarantees the highest quality.

**Team reconfiguration:** An agent team can have only one active team per session, but a team can be disbanded between Phases and a new team formed. When different specialist combinations are needed per Phase, as in the Pipeline pattern, save the previous team's artifacts to files, then tear down the team and create a new one.

Define each agent in `project/.claude/agents/{name}.md`. Required sections: core role, work principles, input/output protocol, error handling, collaboration. In agent team mode, add a `## Team Communication Protocol` section that specifies the message recipients/senders and the scope of task requests.

> For the definition template and full actual files, see "Agent Definition Structure" in `references/agent-design-patterns.md` + `references/team-examples.md`.

**Requirements when including a QA agent:**
- The QA agent must use the `general-purpose` type (`Explore` is read-only and cannot run verification scripts)
- The core of QA is not "existence checks" but **"cross-boundary comparison"** — read the API response and the front-end hook simultaneously and compare their shape
- QA runs not once after everything is complete, but **incrementally right after each module is completed** (incremental QA)
- Detailed guide: see `references/qa-agent-guide.md`

### Phase 4: Skill Creation

Create the skills each agent will use in `project/.claude/skills/{name}/SKILL.md`. For the detailed authoring guide, see `references/skill-writing-guide.md`.

#### 4-0. Existing Skill Overlap Review

Before creating a new skill, check for overlap with the existing skills in `project/.claude/skills/`. As you repeatedly build harnesses, skills with overlapping functionality tend to accumulate under different names.

> For the overlap classification criteria and generalization patterns, see "Skill Reuse Design" in `references/skill-writing-guide.md`.

#### 4-1. Skill Structure

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md (required)
│   ├── YAML frontmatter (name, description required)
│   └── Markdown body
└── Bundled Resources (optional)
    ├── scripts/    - executable code for repetitive/deterministic tasks
    ├── references/ - reference docs loaded conditionally
    └── assets/     - files used in output (templates, images, etc.)
```

#### 4-2. Writing the Description — Actively Inducing Triggers

The description is the skill's only trigger mechanism. Claude tends to judge triggers conservatively, so write the description **actively ("pushy")**.

**Bad example:** `"A skill that processes PDF documents"`
**Good example:** `"Performs all PDF tasks including reading PDF files, extracting text/tables, merging, splitting, rotating, watermarking, encryption, and OCR. Whenever a .pdf file is mentioned or a PDF artifact is requested, you must use this skill."`

Key: describe both what the skill does and the specific trigger situations, and write it so it is distinguished from similar cases that should not trigger.

#### 4-3. Body Writing Principles

| Principle | Description |
|------|------|
| **Explain the Why** | Instead of coercive directives like "ALWAYS/NEVER," convey the reason it should be done that way. When an LLM understands the reason, it judges correctly even in edge cases. |
| **Keep it lean** | The context window is a public good. Aim for the SKILL.md body to stay within 500 lines, and delete or move to references/ any content that does not earn its weight. |
| **Generalize** | Rather than narrow rules that fit only a specific example, explain the principle so it can handle diverse inputs. No overfitting. |
| **Bundle repetitive code** | When you find scripts that agents commonly write during test runs, bundle them in advance into `scripts/`. |
| **Write imperatively** | Use an imperative/directive tone such as "do X" / "perform X." |

#### 4-4. Progressive Disclosure

A skill manages context with a 3-stage loading system:

| Stage | Loading time | Size target |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Metadata** (name + description) | Always present in context | ~100 words |
| **SKILL.md body** | When the skill is triggered | <500 lines |
| **references/** | Only when needed | Unlimited (scripts can run without loading) |

**Size management rules:**
- When SKILL.md approaches 500 lines, split the details into references/ and leave a pointer in the body for "when to read this file"
- Include a **table of contents (ToC)** at the top of reference files of 300 lines or more
- When there are domain/framework-specific variants, split them by domain under references/ so only the relevant files are loaded

```
cloud-deploy/
├── SKILL.md (workflow + selection guide)
└── references/
    ├── aws.md    ← loaded only when AWS is selected
    ├── gcp.md
    └── azure.md
```

#### 4-5. Skill-Agent Linking Principles

- One agent ↔ 1–N skills (1:1 or 1:many)
- A skill shared by multiple agents is also possible
- A skill holds "how it is done," and an agent holds "who does it"

> For detailed authoring patterns, examples, and data schema standards, see `references/skill-writing-guide.md`.

### Phase 5: Integration and Orchestration

The orchestrator is a special form of skill that weaves individual agents and skills into a single workflow and coordinates the whole team. Where the individual skills created in Phase 4 define "what each agent does and how," the orchestrator defines "who collaborates, when, and in what order." For a concrete template, see `references/orchestrator-template.md`.

**Modifying the orchestrator for an existing extension:** For an existing extension rather than a new build, do not create a new orchestrator; modify the existing one. When adding an agent, reflect the new agent in the team composition, task allocation, and data flow, and add trigger keywords related to the new agent to the description.

The orchestrator pattern differs according to the execution mode selected in Phase 2-1:

#### 5-0. Orchestrator Patterns (by mode)

**Agent team pattern (default):**
The orchestrator forms a team with `TeamCreate` and allocates work with `TaskCreate`. Team members communicate directly via `SendMessage` and self-coordinate. The leader (orchestrator) monitors progress and synthesizes the results.

```
[Orchestrator/Leader]
    ├── TeamCreate(team_name, members)
    ├── TaskCreate(tasks with dependencies)
    ├── Team members self-coordinate (SendMessage)
    ├── Collect and synthesize results
    └── Clean up the team
```

**Subagent pattern (alternative):**
The orchestrator calls subagents directly with the `Agent` tool. Parallel execution uses `run_in_background: true`, and results are returned only to main. Use this when team communication is unnecessary and you want to reduce overhead.

```
[Orchestrator]
    ├── Agent(agent-1, run_in_background=true)
    ├── Agent(agent-2, run_in_background=true)
    ├── Wait for and collect results
    └── Produce the integrated artifact
```

**Hybrid pattern:**
Mix different modes per Phase. Frequently used combinations:
- **Parallel collection (sub) → consensus integration (team)**: collect independent material in parallel with subagents in Phase 2 → form a team in Phase 3 for discussion- and consensus-based integration
- **Team creation (team) → verification (sub)**: a team produces a draft in Phase 2 → a single subagent independently verifies it in Phase 3
- **Team reconfiguration between Phases**: `TeamDelete` then a new `TeamCreate` per Phase, with subagent calls inserted in between

When choosing hybrid, specify the execution mode of that Phase at the top of each Phase section in the orchestrator (e.g., `**Execution mode:** agent team`).

#### 5-1. Data Passing Protocol

Specify within the orchestrator how data is passed between agents:

| Strategy | Method | Applicable mode | When appropriate |
|------|------|----------|-----------|
| **Message-based** | Direct communication between team members via `SendMessage` | Team | Real-time coordination, feedback exchange, lightweight state passing |
| **Task-based** | Share task state via `TaskCreate`/`TaskUpdate` | Team | Progress tracking, dependency management, requesting the work itself |
| **File-based** | Write and read files at agreed paths | Team + Sub | Large data, structured artifacts, audit trail needed |
| **Return-value-based** | The Agent tool's return message | Sub | Main collects subagent results directly |

**Recommended combination (team mode):** task-based (coordination) + file-based (artifacts) + message-based (real-time communication)
**Recommended combination (sub mode):** return-value-based (result collection) + file-based (large artifacts)
**Hybrid:** apply the appropriate combination according to each Phase's execution mode

Rules for file-based passing:
- Create a `_workspace/` folder under the working directory to store intermediate artifacts
- File-name convention: `{phase}_{agent}_{artifact}.{ext}` (e.g., `01_analyst_requirements.md`)
- Output only the final artifact to the user-specified path; preserve intermediate files (`_workspace/`) (for post-hoc verification and audit trail)

#### 5-2. Error Handling

Include the error-handling policy within the orchestrator. Core principle: retry once and, if it fails again, proceed without that result (noting the omission in the report); do not delete conflicting data but annotate it with its source.

> For the per-error-type strategy table and implementation details, see "Error Handling" in `references/orchestrator-template.md`.

#### 5-3. Team Size Guidelines

| Work scale | Recommended team size | Tasks per member |
|----------|------------|--------------|
| Small (5–10 tasks) | 2–3 | 3–5 |
| Medium (10–20 tasks) | 3–5 | 4–6 |
| Large (20+ tasks) | 5–7 | 4–5 |

> The more team members, the greater the coordination overhead. Three focused members are better than five distracted ones.

#### 5-4. Registering the CLAUDE.md Harness Pointer

After completing the harness setup, register a minimal pointer in the project's `CLAUDE.md`. Since CLAUDE.md is loaded in every new session, recording only the harness's existence and trigger rules lets the orchestrator skill handle the rest.

**CLAUDE.md template:**

````markdown
## Harness: {domain name}

**Goal:** {one line on the harness's core goal}

**Trigger:** When work related to {domain} is requested, use the `{orchestrator-skill-name}` skill. Simple questions can be answered directly.

**Change Log:**
| Date | Change | Target | Reason |
|------|----------|------|------|
| {YYYY-MM-DD} | Initial setup | All | - |
````

**What not to put in CLAUDE.md:** the agent list, the skill list, the directory structure, the detailed execution rules. Reasons: the agent/skill lists are managed by the orchestrator skill and in `.claude/agents/`, `.claude/skills/`, so they would be duplicated. The directory structure can be checked directly in the file system. CLAUDE.md holds only the **pointer (trigger rules) + Change Log**.

#### 5-5. Follow-up Work Support

The orchestrator must handle not only the initial run but also follow-up work. Guarantee the following three:

**1. Include follow-up keywords in the orchestrator description:**
The initial creation keywords alone will not trigger follow-up requests. Follow-up expressions to include in the description without fail:
- "run again", "rerun", "update", "modify", "supplement"
- "redo only the {subtask} of {domain}"
- "based on the previous result", "improve the result"

**2. Add a context-check step to the orchestrator's Phase 1:**
At the start of the workflow, check whether existing artifacts exist to decide the execution mode:
- `_workspace/` exists + user requests a partial fix → **partial rerun** (re-invoke only that agent)
- `_workspace/` exists + user provides new input → **new run** (move the existing _workspace to `_workspace_prev/`)
- `_workspace/` does not exist → **initial run**

**3. Include re-invocation guidance in the agent definitions:**
Specify in each agent `.md` file the "behavior when previous artifacts exist":
- If a previous result file exists, read it and reflect improvement points
- If user feedback is given, fix only that part

> See the "Phase 0: Context Check" section of the orchestrator template: `references/orchestrator-template.md`

### Phase 6: Verification and Testing

Verify the generated harness. For the detailed testing methodology, see `references/skill-testing-guide.md`.

#### 6-1. Structure Verification

- Verify that all agent files are in the correct location
- Verify the skills' frontmatter (name, description)
- Verify reference consistency between agents
- Verify that no commands were created

#### 6-2. Verification by Execution Mode

- **Agent team**: verify the communication paths between team members, task dependencies, and team-size appropriateness
- **Subagent**: verify each agent's input/output wiring, the `run_in_background` setting, and the return-value collection logic
- **Hybrid**: verify that each Phase's execution mode is specified in the orchestrator and that data passing is not broken at Phase boundaries (that the team's artifact connects to the sub's input on a team → sub transition)

#### 6-3. Skill Execution Test

Perform an actual execution test for each generated skill:

1. **Write test prompts** — write 2–3 realistic test prompts for each skill. Write them as specific, natural sentences that an actual user might enter.

2. **Run a With-skill vs Without-skill comparison** — where possible, run the with-skill and without-skill executions in parallel to confirm the skill's added value. Spawn two agents each:
   - **With-skill**: read the skill and perform the work
   - **Without-skill (baseline)**: perform the same prompt without the skill

3. **Evaluate the results** — evaluate the artifact's quality both qualitatively (user review) and quantitatively (assertion-based). When the artifact is objectively verifiable (file creation, data extraction, etc.), define assertions; when it is subjective (style, design), rely on user feedback.

4. **Iterative improvement loop** — when problems are found in the test results:
   - **Generalize** the feedback and fix the skill (no narrow fix that fits only a specific example)
   - Retest after fixing
   - Repeat until the user is satisfied or there is no longer any meaningful improvement

5. **Bundle repetitive patterns** — when you find code that agents commonly write during test runs (e.g., generating the same helper script in every test), bundle that code in advance into `scripts/`.

#### 6-4. Trigger Verification

Verify that each skill's description triggers correctly:

1. **Should-trigger queries** (8–10) — diverse expressions that should trigger the skill (formal/casual, explicit/implicit)
2. **Should-NOT-trigger queries** (8–10) — "near-miss" queries where the keywords are similar but a different tool/skill, not this one, is appropriate

**Key to writing near-misses:** an obviously unrelated query like "write a Fibonacci function" has no test value. A **query with an ambiguous boundary** like "extract the chart in this Excel file as a PNG" (xlsx skill vs image conversion) is a good test case.

Also check for trigger conflicts with existing skills at this stage.

#### 6-5. Dry-run Test

- Review whether the orchestrator skill's Phase order is logical
- Verify that there are no empty stretches (dead links) in the data-passing path
- Verify that every agent's input matches the previous Phase's output
- Verify that the fallback path for each error scenario is executable

#### 6-6. Writing Test Scenarios

- Add a `## Test Scenarios` section to the orchestrator skill
- Describe at least one normal flow + one error flow

### Phase 7: Harness Evolution

A harness is not a static artifact made once and done. It is a system that keeps evolving according to user feedback.

#### 7-1. Collecting Feedback After a Run

After every harness run completes, request feedback from the user:
- "Is there anything to improve in the result?"
- "Is there anything you'd like to change in the agent team composition or the workflow?"

If there is no feedback, move on. Do not force it, but always provide the opportunity.

#### 7-2. Feedback Reflection Paths

The modification target differs by feedback type:

| Feedback type | Modification target | Example |
|-----------|----------|------|
| Output quality | That agent's skill | "The analysis is too shallow" → add depth criteria to the skill |
| Agent role | Agent definition `.md` | "Security review is also needed" → add a new agent |
| Workflow order | Orchestrator skill | "Verification should come first" → change the Phase order |
| Team composition | Orchestrator + agent | "These two could be merged" → merge the agents |
| Missing trigger | Skill description | "It doesn't work when I phrase it this way" → expand the description |

#### 7-3. Change Log

Record every change in the **Change Log** table of CLAUDE.md (the same table as the "Change Log" section of the Phase 5-4 template):

```markdown
**Change Log:**
| Date | Change | Target | Reason |
|------|----------|------|------|
| 2026-04-05 | Initial setup | All | - |
| 2026-04-07 | Added QA agent | agents/qa.md | Feedback on insufficient artifact quality verification |
| 2026-04-10 | Added tone guide | skills/content-creator | "Too stiff" feedback |
```

Through this log, track which direction the harness has evolved and prevent regression.

#### 7-4. Evolution Triggers

Propose evolution not only when the user explicitly says "modify the harness," but also in the following situations:
- When the same type of feedback recurs two or more times
- When a pattern of repeated agent failure is found
- When the user is observed bypassing the orchestrator and working manually

#### 7-5. Operations/Maintenance Workflow

Systematically perform the inspection, modification, and synchronization of an existing harness. Follow this workflow when Phase 0 enters the "operations/maintenance" branch.

**Step 1: Status Audit**
- Compare the `.claude/agents/` file list with the orchestrator skill's agent composition → produce a mismatch list
- Compare the `.claude/skills/` directory list with the orchestrator skill's skill composition → produce a mismatch list
- Report the audit results to the user

**Step 2: Incremental Addition/Modification**
- Per the user's request, add/modify/delete agents and add/modify/delete skills
- Make changes one at a time, and immediately run Step 3 (synchronization) after each change

**Step 3: Update the CLAUDE.md Change Log**
- Record the date, change, target, and reason in the Change Log table

**Step 4: Verify the Change**
- Structure-verify the modified agent/skill (per Phase 6-1)
- If the modification scope affects triggers, run trigger verification (per Phase 6-4)
- For large changes (architecture change, adding/deleting 3 or more agents), also perform Phase 6-3 (execution test) and 6-5 (dry-run)
- Finally verify the consistency between CLAUDE.md and the actual files

## Deliverable Checklist

Confirm after creation is complete:

- [ ] `project/.claude/agents/` — **agent definition files must be created** (file creation required even for built-in types)
- [ ] `project/.claude/skills/` — skill files (SKILL.md + references/)
- [ ] One orchestrator skill (including data flow + error handling + test scenarios)
- [ ] Execution mode specified (choose among agent team / subagent / hybrid; for hybrid, record the per-Phase mode)
- [ ] `model: "opus"` parameter specified on all Agent calls
- [ ] Existing agent overlap review completed before creating a new agent (Phase 3-0)
- [ ] Existing skill overlap review completed before creating a new skill (Phase 4-0)
- [ ] `.claude/commands/` — nothing created
- [ ] No conflicts with existing agents/skills
- [ ] Skill description written actively ("pushy") — **includes follow-up work keywords**
- [ ] SKILL.md body within 500 lines; split into references/ if exceeded
- [ ] Execution verified with 2–3 test prompts
- [ ] Trigger verification (should-trigger + should-NOT-trigger) completed
- [ ] **Harness pointer registered in CLAUDE.md** (trigger rules + Change Log)
- [ ] **Agent/skill add/delete/modify recorded in the CLAUDE.md Change Log**
- [ ] **Context-check step in the orchestrator's Phase 1** (distinguishing initial/follow-up/partial rerun)

## References

- Harness patterns: `references/agent-design-patterns.md`
- Existing harness examples (including full actual files): `references/team-examples.md`
- Orchestrator template: `references/orchestrator-template.md`
- **Skill writing guide**: `references/skill-writing-guide.md` — authoring patterns, examples, data schema standards
- **Skill testing guide**: `references/skill-testing-guide.md` — testing/evaluation/iterative-improvement methodology
- **QA agent guide**: `references/qa-agent-guide.md` — reference when including a QA agent in a build harness. Includes the integration-consistency verification methodology, boundary bug patterns, and a QA agent definition template. Based on 7 bug cases found in an actual project.

name: heavy-thinking
body:
# Heavy Thinking

## Purpose

Use this skill to spend extra test-time compute on hard problems by running independent reasoning attempts in parallel, then synthesizing them through a critical deliberation pass.

The core pattern is:

```text
problem -> K independent thinkers -> serialized memory cache -> deliberator -> final answer
```

## Activation

Use this skill when at least one condition holds:

- The user explicitly asks for `$heavy-thinking`, "heavy thinking", "parallel reasoning", "multi-agent deliberation", or independent attempts.
- The task is complex, ambiguous, or high impact enough that one reasoning path is brittle.
- Correctness can be checked through tests, source code, logs, math, citations, benchmarks, or other evidence.
- The task benefits from diverse strategies, such as debugging, architecture design, algorithmic reasoning, research synthesis, code review, or implementation planning.

Do not use this skill for simple factual answers, formatting, translation, one-line shell commands, routine edits, or tasks where extra parallel work adds cost without reducing risk.

## Workflow

### 1. Classify the Task

Decide whether the task is:

- `verifiable`: code, math, tests, logs, exact citations, structured data.
- `design`: architecture, API shape, TUI/frontend design, migration planning.
- `synthesis`: paper explanation, comparison, research summary.
- `preference`: subjective writing or style choices.

Prefer this skill for `verifiable`, `design`, and `synthesis` tasks. Use it cautiously for `preference` tasks because averaging multiple answers can weaken style.

### 2. Choose Breadth

Default to `K=3` independent thinkers. Use `K=4` or more only when the user explicitly asks for depth or the problem has high uncertainty.

If subagents are available and the user has authorized parallel agent work, spawn independent subagents. Otherwise, simulate independent passes locally by separating assumptions and strategies before deliberation.

Each thinker must work from the same problem statement but should use a different strategy. Examples:

- code debugging: call-path trace, test/repro focus, config/runtime focus.
- architecture: minimal patch, module-boundary refactor, reference-implementation alignment.
- math/reasoning: algebraic, constructive, contradiction, edge-case analysis.
- research: method, evidence/results, limitations/claims audit.

### 3. Keep Attempts Independent

Do not let thinkers see each other's outputs. Ask each thinker for:

- answer or proposed fix.
- evidence.
- assumptions.
- uncertainty and failure modes.

For coding tasks, give each worker a disjoint write scope when edits are delegated. For read-only analysis, ask for concrete file paths, lines, commands, or observed outputs.

### 4. Build the Memory Cache

After collecting attempts, create a compact cache:

```text
Attempt A:
- conclusion:
- evidence:
- assumptions:
- risks:

Attempt B:
...
```

Prune repetitive reasoning. Preserve exact repro strings, error messages, commands, numbers, file paths, citations, and test outputs. Shuffle or neutralize ordering when the first attempt might otherwise anchor the decision.

### 5. Deliberate

Critically evaluate the cache:

- Identify agreements and disagreements.
- Check which conclusions are supported by evidence.
- Prefer verified facts over plausible narratives.
- Do not majority-vote blindly; a minority attempt can be right.
- If all attempts are flawed, re-derive from the original problem and evidence.
- For code, inspect or test the decisive boundary yourself before finalizing when feasible.

### 6. Act

If the user's request implies implementation, make the chosen change after deliberation. Do not stop at analysis unless the user requested planning only.

For coding work:

- keep edits scoped to the selected solution.
- preserve unrelated user changes.
- add or run verification at the owning boundary.
- integrate useful subagent findings without copying unsupported claims.

### 7. Final Response

Return the final answer or completed-work summary. Include only the useful conclusion and verification. Do not expose the full internal deliberation unless the user asks for the comparison.

For reviews or audits, lead with verified findings. For implementation, report changed files and commands run.

## Failure Modes

- `False consensus`: Multiple attempts repeat the same mistaken assumption. Re-check the original source or repro.
- `Verbose trajectory bias`: Longer reasoning is not necessarily better. Prefer evidence density.
- `Position bias`: Do not trust the first or most polished attempt by default.
- `Preference dilution`: For subjective writing or UI tone, deliberation can flatten distinctive choices.
- `Context overload`: Too many trajectories can bury the signal. Prune aggressively.

## Output Constraint

The user should receive the synthesized result, not the scaffolding. Mention heavy-thinking only when it explains the process or the user explicitly invoked it.

name: high-risk-refactor-planning
description: Use when the user wants a refactor that changes responsibilities, public interfaces, module boundaries, dependency direction, shared state, lifecycle, or other architectural structure. This skill plans and slices the work; it does NOT execute the full refactor by default. Sibling of `low-risk-local-refactoring` (single file) and `medium-risk-structural-refactoring` (multi-file, module-local). For routing by risk, use `refactoring-router`.
body:
# High Risk Refactor Planning

This skill is for architectural refactoring work where boundary judgment matters more than coding speed.

Default behavior: plan first. Do not execute the full refactor under this skill unless the user explicitly approves a narrowed follow-on slice.

Planning artifacts, boundary maps, diagnosis, and migration phases are the default output. Do not perform boundary-changing code edits under this skill without explicit user approval for a single narrowed slice.

## Use When

Use this skill when any of the following are true:

- responsibilities are moving across classes, modules, or services
- public APIs, widely used signatures, or shared contracts may change
- dependency direction, ownership, lifecycle, initialization order, or state boundaries may change
- new abstraction layers may be introduced
- the work touches concurrency, caching, I/O, security, transactions, serialization, or framework registration behavior
- correctness depends on migration order or cross-module reasoning
- the named transformation falls under the **high-risk** tier in `refactoring-router/references/named-refactorings.md` (cross-module Move, Replace Conditional with Polymorphism, Branch by Abstraction, Parallel Change, public Change Function Signature, etc.)

## Do

- map the current boundaries, responsibilities, and dependency directions
- identify why the current design is failing
- define the target boundary changes in plain language
- break the work into small, reviewable migration slices
- identify which slices are low-risk or medium-risk enough to hand off later
- define verification strategy, rollback points, and migration hazards
- prefer named migration patterns over ad-hoc phasing:
  - **Branch by Abstraction** — introduce an abstraction layer in front of the old implementation, migrate callers to the abstraction, swap in the new implementation, retire the old path.
  - **Parallel Change** (also called Expand-Contract) — add the new shape alongside the old one, deprecate the old, migrate callers one-by-one, then remove the old shape.
  Each phase MUST leave the system green at every commit. If a slice cannot be expressed as one step of either pattern, the slice is too big — split further.

## Do Not

- do not perform the whole refactor as one editing pass
- do not hide architectural changes inside "cleanup" language
- do not assume a new abstraction is justified just because it looks cleaner
- do not schedule migration steps that leave the system half-broken between commits
- do not treat missing tests as a reason to improvise aggressively
- do not assume a smaller patch is a safer patch if it still depends on later migration steps
- do not rely on language-level private visibility as proof that a boundary is not shared

## Workflow

1. Explore only the code needed to understand the relevant boundaries, contracts, and pressure points. Do not drift into implementation edits.
2. Produce a diagnosis:
   - what structural problem exists now
   - which current boundaries are failing
   - which risks make this high-risk rather than medium-risk
3. Produce a target design:
   - what ownership, contract, dependency, or lifecycle boundaries should exist instead
   - what must remain compatible during migration
4. Produce migration slices with phases or commits that each leave the system working.
5. For each phase, label it as:
   - planning only
   - executable with `medium-risk-structural-refactoring`
   - executable with `low-risk-local-refactoring`
6. A phase may be handed down only if its correctness no longer depends on later migration steps and it does not temporarily change a shared boundary.
7. Define verification for each phase, including where broader integration checks, compatibility checks, or rollout safeguards are required. If a phase touches behavior whose externally observable shape has no test today, the phase's first slice MUST be "write a characterization test that pins current behavior" (route through `tdd`) — landing green on its own commit before the structural slice. Do not let "no tests exist here" become an excuse to phase a refactor without a tripwire.
8. If this skill creates or updates repository artifacts such as planning docs for the approved work, commit and push those artifact changes as a separate focused change.
9. Stop after the plan unless the user explicitly asks to execute one approved slice.

## Common Pitfalls

- Extracting a common abstraction too early often hides important domain differences.
- Moving methods across classes changes ownership semantics even if the code body stays the same.
- Signature cleanup is usually a migration problem, not a one-shot refactor.
- State and lifecycle refactors fail quietly when initialization order or cleanup timing changes.
- A slice that is small in code size can still be unsafe if it only makes sense after future migration steps land.
- Language-level private symbols may still be part of a shared boundary when frameworks or conventions discover them indirectly.

## Required Output

Provide:

- problem statement
- current boundaries and why they are failing
- target boundaries
- main risks
- phased migration plan
- verification strategy
- rollback or containment points
- which slices are safe to hand to lower-risk skills
- commit message (if repository artifacts were created or updated)
- pushed branch (if repository artifacts were created or updated)

If the user wants this work filed as a GitHub issue, RFC, or written-up plan a team can pick up later, hand off to `request-refactor-plan` for the user interview and issue filing — feed the diagnosis, target boundaries, and migration slices produced here into that skill's Decision Document and Commits sections rather than re-deriving them.

name: improve-codebase-architecture
body:
# Improve Codebase Architecture

Explore a codebase like an AI would, surface architectural friction, discover opportunities for improving testability, and propose module-deepening refactors as GitHub issue RFCs.

A **deep module** (John Ousterhout, "A Philosophy of Software Design") has a small interface hiding a large implementation. Deep modules are more testable, more AI-navigable, and let you test at the boundary instead of inside.

## Process

### 1. Explore the codebase

Use the Agent tool with subagent_type=Explore to navigate the codebase naturally. Do NOT follow rigid heuristics — explore organically and note where you experience friction:

- Where does understanding one concept require bouncing between many small files?
- Where are modules so shallow that the interface is nearly as complex as the implementation?
- Where have pure functions been extracted just for testability, but the real bugs hide in how they're called?
- Where do tightly-coupled modules create integration risk in the seams between them?
- Which parts of the codebase are untested, or hard to test?

The friction you encounter IS the signal.

### 2. Present candidates

Present a numbered list of deepening opportunities. For each candidate, show:

- **Cluster**: Which modules/concepts are involved
- **Why they're coupled**: Shared types, call patterns, co-ownership of a concept
- **Dependency category**: See [REFERENCE.md](REFERENCE.md) for the four categories
- **Test impact**: What existing tests would be replaced by boundary tests

Do NOT propose interfaces yet. Ask the user: "Which of these would you like to explore?"

### 3. User picks a candidate

### 4. Frame the problem space

Before spawning sub-agents, write a user-facing explanation of the problem space for the chosen candidate:

- The constraints any new interface would need to satisfy
- The dependencies it would need to rely on
- A rough illustrative code sketch to make the constraints concrete — this is not a proposal, just a way to ground the constraints

Show this to the user, then immediately proceed to Step 5. The user reads and thinks about the problem while the sub-agents work in parallel.

### 5. Design multiple interfaces

Spawn 3+ sub-agents in parallel using the Agent tool. Each must produce a **radically different** interface for the deepened module.

Prompt each sub-agent with a separate technical brief (file paths, coupling details, dependency category, what's being hidden). This brief is independent of the user-facing explanation in Step 4. Give each agent a different design constraint:

- Agent 1: "Minimize the interface — aim for 1-3 entry points max"
- Agent 2: "Maximize flexibility — support many use cases and extension"
- Agent 3: "Optimize for the most common caller — make the default case trivial"
- Agent 4 (if applicable): "Design around the ports & adapters pattern for cross-boundary dependencies"

Each sub-agent outputs:

1. Interface signature (types, methods, params)
2. Usage example showing how callers use it
3. What complexity it hides internally
4. Dependency strategy (how deps are handled — see [REFERENCE.md](REFERENCE.md))
5. Trade-offs

Present designs sequentially, then compare them in prose.

After comparing, give your own recommendation: which design you think is strongest and why. If elements from different designs would combine well, propose a hybrid. Be opinionated — the user wants a strong read, not just a menu.

### 6. User picks an interface (or accepts recommendation)

### 7. Create GitHub issue

Create a refactor RFC as a GitHub issue using `gh issue create`. Use the template in [REFERENCE.md](REFERENCE.md). Do NOT ask the user to review before creating — just create it and share the URL.

name: insight
description: Use when the user wants a deep-insight report from their AI coding tool session history (Claude Code, Codex, or Cursor). Triggers include "analyze my sessions", "generate insights", "show usage stats", "inspect recent sessions", "cc-session insights", "session trends", "what have I been working on", "summarize my activity", or any data-driven reflection on AI coding tool usage. Mentions of "cc-session", "codex", or "cursor" in an analysis context also trigger. Output language defaults to Chinese.
body:
# AI Coding Tool Session Insight Generator

Generate structured, data-driven insight reports from Claude Code, Codex, and
Cursor session data.

## Response Language

Use **Chinese** for all narrative output (insights, analysis, conclusions). Keep technical
terms (tool names, CLI flags, project names) in their original form. Use English for code
and CLI commands only.

## Data Collection Pipeline

Execute these steps **sequentially** — each builds on the previous. Use the actual tool
outputs, not cached guesses.

### Step 1: Scope & Quick Stats

Determine the time range. Default: **last 10 days**. User may specify otherwise.

#### Claude Code

```bash
# Get aggregate statistics (scans ALL .jsonl files, not index-dependent)
cc-session statistics
cc-session statistics --format json
```

If `cc-session` is not in PATH, build and run from the crate:
```bash
cd ~/projects/models/cc-session-rs && cargo run -- <subcommand>
```

#### Codex

```bash
# Count history queries
wc -l ~/.codex/history.jsonl

# Count archived sessions
ls ~/.codex/archived_sessions/*.jsonl | wc -l

# Count current sessions
ls ~/.codex/sessions/ | wc -l
```

#### Cursor

```bash
# Count agent transcripts
find ~/.cursor/projects -name "*.jsonl" | wc -l

# Per-project breakdown
find ~/.cursor/projects -name "*.jsonl" | sed 's|.*/projects/||; s|/agent-transcripts.*||' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
```

### Step 2: Discover Recent Sessions

#### Claude Code

`cc-session filter` relies on `sessions-index.json` which may be stale. **Always supplement
with direct filesystem scanning**:

```bash
# Primary: filesystem scan for recent sessions
find ~/.claude/projects/ -name "*.jsonl" -maxdepth 2 -mtime -<N> | wc -l

# Per-project breakdown
find ~/.claude/projects/ -name "*.jsonl" -maxdepth 2 -mtime -<N> \
  | sed 's|.*/projects/||; s|/[^/]*$||' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -30

# cc-session filter (may have incomplete coverage)
cc-session filter --since YYYY-MM-DD --limit 999
```

#### Codex

```bash
# Recent history queries (with dates)
python3 -c "
import json
from datetime import datetime
cutoff = $(date +%s) - <N>*86400
with open('$HOME/.codex/history.jsonl') as f:
    for line in f:
        if line.strip():
            obj = json.loads(line)
            if obj.get('ts', 0) >= cutoff:
                print(datetime.fromtimestamp(obj['ts']).strftime('%Y-%m-%d'), obj.get('text', '')[:60])
"

# Recent archived sessions
find ~/.codex/archived_sessions -name "*.jsonl" -mtime -<N> | wc -l
```

#### Cursor

```bash
# Recent transcripts
find ~/.cursor/projects -name "*.jsonl" -mtime -<N> | wc -l

# Recent per-project
find ~/.cursor/projects -name "*.jsonl" -mtime -<N> \
  | sed 's|.*/projects/||; s|/agent-transcripts.*||' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -20
```

**Why both index and filesystem?** `filter` uses a pre-built index that may miss recent
projects. Direct `find` catches everything but lacks metadata. Cross-referencing gives the
best picture.

### Step 3: Deep Analysis via Python

Run the unified analysis script on all three data sources:

```bash
python3 ~/.claude/skills/insight/scripts/analyze_sessions.py <days>
```

This script produces structured data for **all three tools**:

**Claude Code**:
- Daily activity breakdown (session count per day)
- Project ranking (sessions per project)
- Project category classification (Python/Rust/Go/Translation/Papers/etc.)
- Session size distribution (small/medium/large/huge)
- Subagent vs main session ratio
- Claude Code version distribution

**Codex**:
- History query trends
- Session topics from index
- Archived session analysis (events, tool usage)
- Current session count

**Cursor**:
- Transcript count and entry distribution
- Project ranking
- Tool usage patterns (Read, StrReplace, Shell, Grep, etc.)
- Role distribution (user vs assistant)

**Cross-tool**:
- Total activity comparison
- Project overlap detection
- Primary tool signature per platform

Output is JSON to stdout. Capture and interpret it.

### Step 4: Individual Session Sampling

Load representative sessions to understand content patterns:

#### Claude Code
```bash
cc-session load --file <path-to-jsonl> --format summary -v
cc-session load --file <path-to-jsonl> --format turns
```

#### Codex
```bash
# Sample archived session (largest)
python3 -c "
import glob, os
files = glob.glob('$HOME/.codex/archived_sessions/*.jsonl')
largest = max(files, key=os.path.getsize)
print('Largest:', largest, os.path.getsize(largest)/1024, 'KB')
"

# Inspect first few events
head -20 <archived-session>.jsonl | python3 -m json.tool
```

#### Cursor
```bash
# Sample transcript
head -5 <transcript>.jsonl | python3 -m json.tool
```

Pick 5-10 sessions strategically across all three tools: the largest, a few from top
projects, a few from different categories.

### Step 5: Visual Charts (Claude Code only)

Generate charts when the data warrants visual representation:

```bash
cc-session statistics --chart daily --n-days <N> --chart-output /tmp/cc-daily.png
cc-session statistics --chart tool-usage --chart-output /tmp/cc-tools.png
```

Read the PNG files to include in the report.

## Report Structure

Produce a structured report with these sections. Adapt based on what the data reveals —
not every section needs to be equally deep.

### 1. 总览 (Overview)

Three-tool summary table:

| 工具 | 会话/Transcript 数 | 项目数 | 数据量 | 时间范围 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Claude Code | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Codex | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| Cursor | ... | ... | ... | ... |

### 2. 每日活跃度 (Daily Activity)

Per-tool daily activity with combined timeline. Note:
- Which tool dominates which days
- Burst vs steady patterns per tool
- Gaps that span all tools vs tool-specific gaps

### 3. 项目版图 (Project Landscape)

- **Language/category distribution** (Claude Code data) — table + pie chart description
- **Top 10 projects per tool** — with inferred project purpose
- **Cross-tool project overlap** — which projects appear in multiple tools and why
- **Subagent usage** (Claude Code) — which projects spawn the most agents

### 4. 工具使用模式对比 (Tool Usage Patterns)

Per-tool tool ranking + cross-tool comparison table:

| 维度 | Claude Code | Cursor | Codex |
|---|---|---|---|
| 读取 | Read | Read | (event-based) |
| 编辑 | Edit | StrReplace | apply_patch |
| Shell | Bash | Shell | exec_command |
| 搜索 | Glob + Grep | Grep + Glob | — |
| 编辑:写入比 | ... | ... | ... |

Highlight **tool-specific signatures**:
- Claude Code: Edit:Write ratio reveals incremental vs greenfield habits
- Cursor: StrReplace vs Write ratio reveals editing style
- Codex: exec_command dominance reveals terminal-native workflow

### 5. 会话规模分析 (Session Scale)

Per-tool distribution (small/medium/large/huge) with highlights:
- Codex archived sessions are typically much larger (1-2MB avg) than Cursor (30KB avg)
- Claude Code spans both ends (small subagents to 30MB paper translation sessions)

### 6. 版本轨迹 (Version Trail)

Claude Code version distribution. Codex and Cursor don't expose version in their data.

### 7. 开发者画像 (Developer Profile)

Synthesize **across all three tools** into 3-5 bullet-point character traits. This is the
**most valuable section** — it should reveal how the user **orchestrates multiple AI tools**
rather than using just one.

Key questions to answer:
- Which tool for which task? (e.g., Cursor for code review, Claude Code for deep dev, Codex for quick commands)
- Does project type drive tool choice?
- Are there temporal patterns? (e.g., Codex in morning, Claude Code for deep sessions)

### 8. 发现的问题 (Issues Found)

Any tool limitations, data gaps, or anomalies discovered during analysis.

## Cross-Tool Insight Writing Principles

These distinguish a good multi-tool report from a mediocre one:

1. **Tool choice is a signal.** If the same project appears in both Cursor and Claude Code,
   ask WHY — what does each tool do better for that project?

2. **Ratios tell stories per tool.**
   - Claude Code Edit:Write = 13.2:1 → incremental modifier
   - Cursor StrReplace:Write = 4.9:1 → more file creation
   - Codex exec_command >> everything else → terminal-native operator

3. **Find the surprising overlap.** The 16 projects shared between Claude Code and Cursor
   were not obvious without cross-tool analysis.

4. **Connect temporal patterns across tools.** If Codex spiked in Feb-Mar and Claude Code
   spiked in May, that's not coincidence — it's a migration or workflow shift.

5. **Infer intent from project names across tools.** `cc-plugin-rs` in Cursor = code review
   context. `cc-plugin-rs` in Claude Code = feature implementation context.

6. **Quantify everything.** Don't say "a lot of Rust". Say "Rust: Claude Code 121 (32.9%),
   Cursor 45+ transcripts, Codex 8+ sessions".

7. **Flag anomalies.** Version jumps, tool-specific gaps, unusually large sessions — these
   are either interesting or problematic, and either way worth calling out.

## Edge Cases

- **No recent sessions for a tool**: Report that and check if the tool's data directory
  exists. For Codex: `~/.codex/`. For Cursor: `~/.cursor/`.
- **Very few sessions (<20 total across all tools)**: Widen the time range.
- **All sessions in one project across all tools**: Still analyze — depth of engagement,
  tool preference patterns within that project.
- **cc-session not installed**: Build from `~/projects/models/cc-session-rs` with
  `cargo run --`.

## References

改 CLI、契约或实现时，先改下列 `~/projects/<lang>/<repo>/` 路径，再同步本 skill 正文。

| 用途 | 路径 |
|------|------|
| 仓库根 | `~/projects/models/cc-session-rs` |
| 实现 | `~/projects/models/cc-session-rs/src/` |
| 构建 / 测试 | `~/projects/models/cc-session-rs/justfile` |
| 项目 Agent 说明 | `~/projects/models/cc-session-rs/CLAUDE.md` |
| 本 skill | `~/projects/skills/insight/SKILL.md` |

name: jcode-review-loop
description: Use when the user wants to run the project-specific multi-round review and validation loop for the jcode + jcode_cli pair — review, audit, regression-check, or refine workflow involving jcode, jcode_cli, daemon/client integration, stream-json, resume/fork/session semantics, permission round-trips, hub mode, or any change where Python server behavior must be verified through the Rust CLI entrypoint.
body:
# jcode / jcode_cli Review Loop

ADR-777 是本 skill 的规范真源：

- 原则、边界、枚举、验证策略：看 ADR-777
- 本文件只保留执行步骤与命令模板

Use this skill for cross-repo review work on:

- `$JCODE_ROOT`
- `$JCODE_CLI_ROOT`

Treat them as one product boundary, not two independent repos.

## Core Rule

If a change can affect daemon boot, event streaming, session semantics,
permissions, or hub routing, the authoritative product check is **jcode_cli
E2E**, not `jcode` unit tests alone.

Reason:

1. `jcode_cli` launches or connects to the runtime
2. `jcode_cli` drives `python -m jcode daemon`
3. users experience the result through the Rust CLI, not through isolated
   Python internals

## Preflight

Before any review or fix round, set and validate the environment:

```bash
export JCODE_ROOT=<path-to-python-jcode>
export JCODE_CLI_ROOT=<path-to-crates-jcode_cli>
export JCODE_E2E_PYTHON=<python-for-jcode>
export JCODE_PYTHON="$JCODE_E2E_PYTHON"
```

Only set `JCODE_E2E_MODEL` when you intentionally run real-model exploratory
checks:

```bash
export JCODE_E2E_MODEL=<real-model-id>
```

Then generate a pair manifest and verify the surfaces themselves:

```bash
cargo test --manifest-path "$JCODE_CLI_ROOT/Cargo.toml" -- --list
cargo test --manifest-path "$JCODE_CLI_ROOT/Cargo.toml" --no-run
cd "$JCODE_ROOT" && uv run pytest --collect-only -q src/jcode/tests/...
```

If Python collection fails because of missing deps or bad env, record it as
`infra/test-env failure`. Do not present it as a confirmed code bug.

Important:

- Do not rely on bare `pytest` in `python/jcode`; use explicit test paths.
- Prefer explicit `cargo test --manifest-path ...` from outside the crate.
- Add a minimal cross-repo smoke before the first real round.

## Review Structure

When using `pilot review`, split review goals instead of using one generic
prompt forever.

### Round 1

Run broad review across three surfaces:

1. `jcode` daemon / executor / stream-events / session logic
2. `jcode_cli` launcher / client / json-stream / hub backend
3. cross-repo compatibility: “what breaks at the Rust CLI when Python changes”

Persist XML output for each round.

### Round 2+

Only ask for:

- incomplete fixes
- regressions
- missing tests
- remaining cross-repo protocol risks

Always include:

- confirmed issues from the previous round
- already-fixed issues
- this round's diff
- instruction not to re-report already closed issues without new evidence

### Special Topic Rounds

Use dedicated prompts for:

- E2E testing strategy
- stream event schema compatibility
- session / resume / fork lineage
- permission round-trip
- hub / pub routing

Stop when one of these is true:

- reached `max_rounds`
- two consecutive rounds produced no new confirmed issue
- only exploratory-only risks remain

## Issue Matrix

For this project, every finding should carry:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `issue_id` | stable de-dup id across rounds |
| `severity` | blocking / major / minor |
| `confirmed` | source-verified true / weak / false |
| `surface` | daemon-boot / stream-contract / session-semantics / permission-roundtrip / hub-routing / transport-wire / renderer-only |
| `primary_gate` | primary gate that must accept or reject the finding |
| `secondary_gates` | optional supporting gates |
| `repro_cmd` | shortest reproduction command |
| `evidence` | logs / transcript / file refs / XML refs |
| `round` | discovery round |
| `fixed` | whether the issue has been repaired |

Recommended gate tags:

- `jcode-pytest`
- `jcode-send-msg-e2e`
- `jcode-cli-e2e-json`
- `jcode-cli-e2e-hub`
- `stream-contract-test`
- `rust-unit-only`

## Validation Rules

Validation order:

1. baseline
2. smallest deterministic gate
3. broader deterministic gate
4. real-model exploratory gate

If a baseline already fails, mark it as pre-existing. Do not count it as this
round's regression.

E2E gates may retry once. Two consecutive failures are required before
classifying them.

### If you changed `jcode` internals only

Run the relevant Python tests first:

```bash
cd "$JCODE_ROOT"
uv run pytest <explicit test paths>
```

### If you changed `jcode` daemon / stream / session / permission / hub code

Python tests are not enough. Also run:

```bash
cargo test --manifest-path "$JCODE_CLI_ROOT/Cargo.toml" \
  --test e2e_json_stream -- --nocapture
```

If hub behavior may be affected, also run:

```bash
cargo test --manifest-path "$JCODE_CLI_ROOT/Cargo.toml" \
  --test e2e_hub_smoke -- --nocapture
```

### If you changed `jcode_cli` launcher / client / json-stream / hub backend

Run Rust unit tests plus the same `jcode_cli` E2E suites, because these files
are the product entrypoint.

### If you changed transport / socket / request-correlation behavior

Also run the daemon direct E2E:

```bash
cd "$JCODE_ROOT"
uv run pytest src/jcode/tests/e2e/test_send_msg_daemon.py -v
```

### If you changed stream schema / event contract behavior

Add a protocol-contract layer before broad E2E:

- schema / version check
- transcript or snapshot contract test
- compatibility-focused test if available

### If you changed renderer-only code

Rust unit tests may be sufficient unless the change also touches queueing,
event consumption, permission dialogs, or session display logic.

## Fix Loop

Use the same repair discipline as the general multi-agent review flow:

1. confirm against source
2. fix one real bug at a time
3. keep one bug per commit when committing is requested
4. run the smallest relevant gate immediately
5. rerun broader cross-repo gate before declaring done

## Failure Taxonomy

Every failing test/result must be classified as one of:

- `implementation bug`
- `test bug`
- `infra/test-env failure`

Never collapse them into a single “tests failed” bucket.

Always attach minimum evidence:

- command
- stdout/stderr
- relevant env
- pair manifest id
- transcript/log path for daemon/hub/stream failures

## Output Order

For review results, present:

1. findings / matrix
2. confirmed items
3. fixes
4. validation results
5. remaining risks

Do not lead with file-by-file edits.

name: kami
description: 'Typeset professional documents and product landing pages: resumes, one-pagers, white papers, letters, portfolios, slide decks, landing pages. Warm parchment, ink-blue accent, serif-led hierarchy. CN uses Source Han Serif SC (思源宋体), EN uses Charter, JA uses YuMincho (best-effort). Triggers on "做 PDF / 排版 / 一页纸 / 白皮书 / 作品集 / 简历 / PPT / slides / Marp / markdown slides / マークダウンのスライド / 落地页 / 官网 / landing page / product page", or "build me a resume / make a one-pager / design a slide deck / turn this into a PDF / make this presentable / create a landing page".'
body:
# kami · 紙

**紙 · かみ** - the paper your deliverables land on.

Good content deserves good paper. One design language across eight document types: warm parchment canvas, ink-blue accent, serif-led hierarchy, tight editorial rhythm.

Part of `Kaku · Waza · Kami` - Kaku writes code, Waza drills habits, **Kami delivers documents**.

## Step 0 · Load brand profile (if exists)

Check `~/.config/kami/brand.md` (preferred) or `~/.kami/brand.md` (legacy fallback). If found, read `references/brand-profile.md` for the full four-layer application spec (placeholder substitution, session defaults, visual customization, habit notes) and its six guardrails. If no profile exists, continue without interruption.

Key rule: explicit prompt > editorial judgment > habit notes > frontmatter defaults > built-in defaults. Profile fills gaps silently; it never overrides the current conversation.

## Step 0.5 · User project style scan (opt-in)

Run this only when the user explicitly references a sibling project as a visual reference: "like my <project> site", "match the style of <repo>", "use the look from <directory>". Skip silently when no such reference exists.

When triggered, before generating:

1. Locate the referenced project's style files:
   ```bash
   find <referenced-path> -maxdepth 4 \( -name "*.css" -o -name "tailwind.config.*" -o -name "theme.*" -o -name "tokens.*" \) | head -20
   ```
2. Extract: dominant color values (hex / hsl), font stack, spacing scale, border-radius scale. Prefer values declared in CSS variables or design tokens over inline literals.
3. Merge into the in-session brand profile as Layer C (visual customization), not Layer B (session defaults). Do not override an explicit `--brand` flag or values that the user typed in this turn.
4. Report back in one line before continuing: "scanned <project>, extracted N colors / M fonts; using as visual reference."

Skip and fall back to the brand profile defaults if the referenced path does not exist, no CSS-like files are found, or the extraction would conflict with the user's explicit values in the current message.

---

## Step 1 · Decide the language

**Match the user's language.** Chinese -> `*.html` / `slides-weasy.html`. English -> `*-en.html` / `slides-weasy-en.html`. Japanese -> CJK path (`.html` / `slides-weasy.html`) as best-effort, JP Mincho first, visual QA before shipping. Korean -> dedicated `*-ko.html` / `slides-weasy-ko.html` family as best-effort, visual QA before shipping. Reference docs are shared English specs.

When ambiguous (e.g. a one-word command like "resume"), ask a one-liner rather than guess.

| User language | HTML templates | Slides (PDF default) | Slides (PPTX fallback) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chinese (primary) | `*.html` | `slides-weasy.html` | `slides.py` |
| English | `*-en.html` | `slides-weasy-en.html` | `slides-en.py` |
| Japanese (best-effort) | `*.html` | `slides-weasy.html` | `slides.py` |
| Korean (best-effort) | `*-ko.html` | `slides-weasy-ko.html` | n/a (use `slides-en.py` only if PPTX is required) |
| Other languages (best-effort) | choose CJK or EN path by script coverage, then verify manually | choose `slides-weasy.html` or `slides-weasy-en.html`, then verify manually | use `slides.py` / `slides-en.py` only if PPTX is required |

> Default to the WeasyPrint HTML path; fall back to PPTX (`slides*.py`) only when the user explicitly needs an editable deck.

Always use `CHEATSHEET.md` and `references/*.md` for design, writing, production, and diagram guidance.

Code blocks with `class="language-*"` are highlighted only when optional `Pygments` is installed in the build environment. Without it, PDFs still render and code blocks stay monochrome.

## Step 1.5 · Intent extraction (silent checklist)

Before choosing a template, verify these four dimensions are clear. Do not ask unless 2+ are missing and cannot be inferred from context.

| Dimension | What to extract | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Why this document exists | Persuade investor vs. align internal team vs. close a candidate |
| **Audience** | Who reads it, what they already know | Technical CTO (skip basics) vs. non-technical board (explain terms) |
| **Constraint** | Hard limits on length, format, tone, or delivery | "One page max", "formal English", "print-ready A4" |
| **Success** | What outcome counts as success | They schedule a meeting / they approve the budget / they understand the architecture |

Rules:
- If the conversation already answered a dimension, skip it silently.
- If a dimension can be inferred from the document type (e.g. resume purpose is always "get an interview"), skip it.
- If 2+ dimensions are genuinely unclear, ask in a single compact question (max 2 sub-questions).
- Never ask all four as a checklist. This is a background verification, not a form.

## Execution contract

Before creating or modifying an output, lock the contract: language, template, output format, page or length target, visual acceptance check, and verification command. Infer from the user's request when clear; ask only when missing fields materially change the deliverable.

Use the nearest existing template and verification path. Do not add a new template, stabilizer profile, shared CSS layer, dependency, script flag, or optional mode unless the current request cannot be satisfied without it.
