When YouTube automation behaves oddly, run the case with daemon trace enabled
so the exact CDP calls, page navigations, screenshots, and breadcrumbs survive
after the script exits:

```bash
TRACE_DIR="$(mktemp -d /tmp/bh-youtube-trace.XXXXXX)"
BH_TRACE=1 BH_TRACE_DIR="$TRACE_DIR" browser-harness --reload
BH_TRACE=1 BH_TRACE_DIR="$TRACE_DIR" browser-harness -c '
function trace_note(text, extra) {
  return __cdp_native_meta("trace_note", JSON.stringify({text, extra: extra || {}}))
}
trace_note("youtube:start", {case:"search-watch"})
new_tab("https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=" + encodeURIComponent("OpenAI"))
wait_for_load(20)
wait(3)
print(JSON.stringify(page_info()))
trace_note("youtube:page-info", page_info())
print("screenshot=" + capture_screenshot(null, false, 1600))
print(__cdp_native_meta("trace_status", null))
'
BH_TRACE_DIR="$TRACE_DIR" browser-harness --trace ls
BH_TRACE_DIR="$TRACE_DIR" browser-harness --trace show <trace-prefix-or-path>
```

Notes from current CLI behavior:

- `BH_TRACE` or `BH_TRACE_DIR` must be set before the daemon generation starts.
  If a daemon is already running without trace, run `browser-harness --reload`
  with the same trace environment, then retry the script.
- `--trace ls` can list a live trace before shutdown; it may mark the file as
  `(crash)` until the daemon exits cleanly and writes `INDEX.jsonl`.
- Add `trace_note` breadcrumbs around high-risk actions such as opening `Ask`,
  submitting a question, clicking transcript controls, and selecting a candidate
  video. They make later `--trace show` review much faster.

## Ground Rules

- Prefer `new_tab(url)` so the user's current tab is not clobbered.
- After navigation, verify with `page_info()` and a screenshot.
- Extract structured DOM text; do not rely on OCR unless DOM extraction fails.
- Before re-analyzing a watch URL, check `/Users/junix/youtube-talks` for the
  video ID or URL. Reuse or update the existing note when present.
- YouTube text may lazy-load. Use `wait_for_load()`, then `wait(3)` to
  `wait(5)` before scraping a search or watch page.
- Do not claim the video content itself beyond what YouTube's visible metadata,
  transcript, chapters, or Ask panel returned.
- Do not download or rehost copyrighted video/audio unless the user explicitly
  asks and the use is legitimate.

## Source Priority For Understanding A Video

When the user asks what a video says, prioritize sources in this order:

1. **Transcript / caption tracks**: most direct textual evidence when present.
2. **YouTube Ask / Gemini panel**: best fallback when transcript is unavailable;
   label answers as coming from YouTube Ask.
3. **Title, channel, description, chapters, pinned comment**: creator/page
   supplied context; good for topic boundaries and follow-up questions.
4. **Viewer comments**: useful for audience concerns, disagreements, field
   experience, corrections, and interesting angles; not reliable as factual
   evidence about the video unless independently verifiable.

Final answers should name the evidence used and uncertainty. For example:

```text
Sources used: page title/metadata, chapters, top comments, YouTube Ask answers.
Transcript was not available through the page, so content claims are based on
YouTube Ask and should be treated as generated summaries.
```

## Preferred Channels

When the user asks to find or try another AI-related YouTube video, check these
channels before broad YouTube search. Do **not** exhaust one channel video by
video just because it is first in the list. First scan across the preferred
channels for videos updated **today** in the user's current timezone; if any
today-updated candidate is not already present under `/Users/junix/youtube-talks`,
visit and analyze one of those first. Only fall back to recent-but-not-today
videos when no preferred channel has an unvisited today-updated video.

Queue-first rule: before opening preferred channels, check the local discovery
queue at `/Users/junix/youtube-talks/_queue/youtube-candidates.jsonl`. If it has
`status:"pending"` records, process the freshest relevant pending video first.
Only refresh preferred channels when the queue has no usable pending candidate.

- Nate B. Jones: https://www.youtube.com/@NateBJones
- Latent Space: https://www.youtube.com/@LatentSpacePod
- AI Upload: https://www.youtube.com/@AIUpload
- antirez: https://www.youtube.com/@antirez
- InfoQ: https://www.youtube.com/@infoq
- Huberman Lab: https://www.youtube.com/@hubermanlab
- Matt Pocock: https://www.youtube.com/@mattpocockuk
- Y Combinator: https://www.youtube.com/@ycombinator
- Theo - t3.gg: https://www.youtube.com/@t3dotgg
- Lev Selector: https://www.youtube.com/@lev-selector
- a16z: https://www.youtube.com/@a16z
- IndyDevDan: https://www.youtube.com/@indydevdan
- Lex Fridman: https://www.youtube.com/@lexfridman
- Peter Diamandis: https://www.youtube.com/@peterdiamandis
- DevOps Toolbox: https://www.youtube.com/@devopstoolbox
- AI Engineer: https://www.youtube.com/@aiDotEngineer
- Claude: https://www.youtube.com/@claude
- Devnexus Conference: https://www.youtube.com/@devnexus-conference
- 20VC: https://www.youtube.com/@20VC
- O'Reilly: https://www.youtube.com/@oreilly
- OpenAI: https://www.youtube.com/@OpenAI
- atmo: https://www.youtube.com/@atmoio
- Dwarkesh Patel: https://www.youtube.com/@DwarkeshPatel
- AI Labs: https://www.youtube.com/@AILABS-393
- ThePrimeTimeagen: https://www.youtube.com/@ThePrimeTimeagen
- Ryan Peterman: https://www.youtube.com/@RyanLPeterman
- Databricks: https://www.youtube.com/@Databricks
- In the World of AI: https://www.youtube.com/@intheworldofai

## Discovery Queue

Use a local queue to separate "find videos" from "read videos". This avoids
opening every preferred channel on every `try another` request.

Queue path:

```text
/Users/junix/youtube-talks/_queue/youtube-candidates.jsonl
```

Each line is one JSON object:

```json
{"video_id":"<id>","url":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=<id>","title":"<title>","channel":"<channel>","channel_url":"<channel-url>","discovered_at":"<ISO timestamp>","published_text":"<visible recency>","status":"pending"}
```

Allowed statuses:

- `pending`: discovered but not read yet.
- `processing`: selected for the current run.
- `skipped`: unavailable, members-only, duplicate, irrelevant, or not worth
  reading.
- `stale`: old candidate no longer worth prioritizing.

`try another` selection order:

1. Read the queue and pick a `pending` candidate before opening YouTube
   channels.
2. Prefer candidates whose `published_text` is `minutes ago`, `hours ago`, or a
   date matching today.
3. Before opening, still de-duplicate by `video_id` and URL against
   `/Users/junix/youtube-talks`; remove duplicates from the queue or mark them
   `skipped` if the reason should be preserved.
4. When a video is selected, mark it `processing` conceptually; after writing the
   viewing note, remove its JSONL record from the queue. The Markdown note is
   the durable completion record.
5. If no usable `pending` candidate exists, refresh the queue by scanning
   preferred channels and appending new candidates as `pending`.

Do not create a full viewing note for a candidate during discovery. The queue
record is only a lightweight TODO; the final Markdown note is created only after
the reading workflow succeeds.

Single-channel pass. Replace `CHANNEL_URL` with one preferred channel URL and
visit its `/videos` tab when debugging a specific channel:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
const channel = "CHANNEL_URL".replace(/\/$/, "")
new_tab(channel + "/videos")
wait_for_load(20)
wait(3)
print(JSON.stringify(page_info()))
print(js(`(() => JSON.stringify(
  Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("a[href*=watch]"))
    .map((a, i) => {
      const text = (a.innerText || a.getAttribute("title") || "").trim()
      const card = a.closest("ytd-rich-item-renderer, ytd-grid-video-renderer, ytd-video-renderer") || a.parentElement
      const cardLines = card ? card.innerText.split("\\n").map(s => s.trim()).filter(Boolean) : []
      const cardTitle = cardLines.find(s => !/^\\d{1,2}:\\d{2}(:\\d{2})?$/.test(s)) || text
      return {
        i,
        text,
        title: cardTitle,
        href: a.href,
        videoId: new URL(a.href).searchParams.get("v"),
        cardText: card ? card.innerText.slice(0, 500) : ""
      }
    })
    .filter(x => x.title && x.videoId && !/^\\d{1,2}:\\d{2}(:\\d{2})?$/.test(x.title))
    .slice(0, 30)
))()`))
'
```

Better default for `try another`: scan several preferred channels first, collect
the newest visible candidates, then pick an unvisited video whose metadata says
`today`, `hours ago`, or otherwise matches the current date. This prevents
draining one channel while other preferred channels have fresh videos.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
const channels = [
  "https://www.youtube.com/@NateBJones",
  "https://www.youtube.com/@LatentSpacePod",
  "https://www.youtube.com/@AIUpload",
  "https://www.youtube.com/@OpenAI",
  "https://www.youtube.com/@aiDotEngineer",
  "https://www.youtube.com/@claude",
  "https://www.youtube.com/@t3dotgg"
]
const results = []
for (const channel of channels) {
  new_tab(channel.replace(/\/$/, "") + "/videos")
  wait_for_load(20)
  wait(2)
  const items = JSON.parse(js(`(() => JSON.stringify(
    Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("a[href*=watch]"))
      .map((a, i) => {
        const card = a.closest("ytd-rich-item-renderer, ytd-grid-video-renderer, ytd-video-renderer") || a.parentElement
        const text = (a.innerText || a.getAttribute("title") || "").trim()
        const cardLines = card ? card.innerText.split("\\n").map(s => s.trim()).filter(Boolean) : []
        const cardTitle = cardLines.find(s => !/^\\d{1,2}:\\d{2}(:\\d{2})?$/.test(s)) || text
        return {
          i,
          channel: location.href.replace(/\/videos.*/, ""),
          text,
          title: cardTitle,
          href: a.href,
          videoId: new URL(a.href).searchParams.get("v"),
          cardText: card ? card.innerText.slice(0, 600) : ""
        }
      })
      .filter(x => x.title && x.videoId && !/^\\d{1,2}:\\d{2}(:\\d{2})?$/.test(x.title))
      .slice(0, 10)
  ))()`))
  results.push(...items)
}
print(JSON.stringify(results, null, 2))
'
```

De-duplicate each candidate before opening:

```bash
rg -n '<video-id>|<watch-url>' /Users/junix/youtube-talks
```

Channel extraction trap: a channel `/videos` page often exposes two watch
anchors per card. The first anchor can be only a duration such as `2:35`, while
the second is the real title. Reject duration-only text and derive `title` from
the card lines before choosing a video; otherwise the workflow may open the
right URL but record a useless title.

## Search Videos

Use broad YouTube search only after preferred channels have no unvisited recent
AI-related video, or when the user asks for a different topic/source. Open a
YouTube search page and collect visible video cards.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
new_tab("https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=" + encodeURIComponent("AI"))
wait_for_load(20)
wait(3)
print(JSON.stringify(page_info()))
print(js(`JSON.stringify(
  Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-video-renderer")).slice(0, 10)
    .map((el, i) => [
      i + 1,
      el.querySelector("a#video-title") && el.querySelector("a#video-title").innerText.trim(),
      el.querySelector("a#video-title") && el.querySelector("a#video-title").href,
      el.querySelector("ytd-channel-name a, #channel-info a") &&
        el.querySelector("ytd-channel-name a, #channel-info a").innerText.trim(),
      Array.from(el.querySelectorAll("#metadata-line span"))
        .map(s => s.innerText.trim()).filter(Boolean).join(" | "),
      el.querySelector("ytd-thumbnail-overlay-time-status-renderer span, .ytd-thumbnail-overlay-time-status-renderer") &&
        el.querySelector("ytd-thumbnail-overlay-time-status-renderer span, .ytd-thumbnail-overlay-time-status-renderer").innerText.trim()
    ])
    .filter(v => v[1] && v[2])
)`))
print("screenshot=" + capture_screenshot(null, false, 1600))
'
```

Use `innerText`, not only `textContent`, for YouTube titles. `textContent` can
drop letters in rendered icon/text hybrids.

Search layout trap: YouTube search may render rich result blocks where
`ytd-video-renderer` is empty even though visible watch results exist. If the
main selector returns `null` or `[]`, fall back to visible watch links:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
print(js(`(() => JSON.stringify(
  Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("a[href*=watch]"))
    .map((a, i) => ({
      i,
      text: (a.innerText || a.getAttribute("title") || "").trim(),
      href: a.href,
      videoId: new URL(a.href).searchParams.get("v")
    }))
    .filter(x => x.text && x.videoId)
    .slice(0, 40)
))()`))
'
```

Before recommending videos for deeper analysis, de-duplicate against the local
archive:

```bash
rg -n '<video-id>|<watch-url>' /Users/junix/youtube-talks
```

If a result already exists, report the existing note path and avoid doing a full
Ask/Q&A pass unless the user asks for a refresh.

## Inspect A Watch Page

```bash
browser-harness -c '
new_tab("https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SXneZ3bRKO4")
wait_for_load(20)
wait(5)
print(JSON.stringify(page_info()))
print(js(`JSON.stringify({
  title: document.querySelector("h1 yt-formatted-string, h1.ytd-watch-metadata")?.innerText?.trim() || document.title,
  channel: document.querySelector("ytd-watch-metadata ytd-channel-name a, #owner #channel-name a")?.innerText?.trim() || null,
  metadata: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-watch-info-text #info span, #info-strings yt-formatted-string, #info #info-text span"))
    .map(e => e.innerText.trim()).filter(Boolean).slice(0, 8),
  playerPaused: document.querySelector(".html5-main-video") ? document.querySelector(".html5-main-video").paused : null,
  playerTime: document.querySelector(".ytp-time-current")?.innerText || null,
  playerDuration: document.querySelector(".ytp-time-duration")?.innerText || null,
  buttons: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-watch-metadata button, #top-level-buttons-computed button"))
    .map(e => e.innerText.trim() || e.getAttribute("aria-label") || "")
    .filter(Boolean).slice(0, 12)
})`))
print("screenshot=" + capture_screenshot(null, false, 1600))
'
```

Report visible state plainly: title, channel, view/date metadata if present,
player time/duration, visible buttons, and screenshot path.

Metadata trap: YouTube may expose both rounded and exact values in the same DOM
snapshot, such as `14K views` and `14,726 views`. Prefer exact numeric values
when available, but keep the visible rounded value if that is all the page
exposes. De-duplicate repeated metadata before reporting.

## Single-Video Understanding Workflow

Use this workflow for a requested watch URL:

1. Extract the video ID and check `/Users/junix/youtube-talks` for an existing
   note.
2. Open the video and inspect title/channel/date/views/duration.
3. Try transcript/caption extraction.
4. Read description, chapters, and comments; screen for high-information
   comments rather than copying only the first visible comments.
5. If transcript is missing or incomplete, open `Ask`.
6. Ask `summary` first.
7. Turn chapters/comments/title into focused follow-up questions.
8. Deliver link, title, channel, source list, summary, key insights, comment
   insights, Q&A answers, and uncertainty.
9. If the user wants a durable record, write it under
   `/Users/junix/youtube-talks/YYYY-MM/<Chinese-title>.md` and commit it from
   the `youtube-talks` repo.

Good follow-up prompts:

- `What are the main claims in this video?`
- `List the products, tools, or companies mentioned.`
- `What does the chapter "<chapter title>" explain?`
- `What are the most actionable takeaways?`
- `What are viewers in the comments reacting to?`
- Any question suggested by the Ask panel itself.

## Try Transcript And Caption Tracks

First try the visible transcript UI or caption track metadata. On current
YouTube layouts, `Show transcript` is often hidden inside the collapsed video
description. Before deciding the transcript button is missing, expand the
description/details area by clicking visible `...more`, `more`, or `Show more`
controls, then search again. If a transcript button is visible, click it and
scrape `ytd-transcript-segment-renderer`.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
print(js(`JSON.stringify({
  moreButtons: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("button, tp-yt-paper-button, yt-button-shape button, yt-formatted-string, div, span"))
    .map((e, i) => ({i, text:(e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim(), aria:e.getAttribute("aria-label") || ""}))
    .filter(x => /^(\\.\\.\\.more|more|show more)$/i.test(x.text) || /^show more$/i.test(x.aria))
    .slice(0, 20),
  transcriptButtons: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("button, tp-yt-paper-button, yt-button-shape button"))
    .map((e, i) => ({i, text:(e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim(), aria:e.getAttribute("aria-label") || ""}))
    .filter(x => /transcript/i.test(x.text + " " + x.aria)),
  captionTracks: (() => {
    const scripts = Array.from(document.scripts).map(s => s.textContent || "").join("\\n")
    const m = scripts.match(/"captionTracks":(\\[.*?\\])/)
    return m ? m[1].slice(0, 1200) : null
  })()
})`))
'
```

If `moreButtons` contains a visible description expander, click it before the
transcript pass:

```bash
browser-harness --debug-clicks -c '
const result = js(`(() => {
  const candidates = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("button, tp-yt-paper-button, yt-button-shape button, yt-formatted-string, div, span"))
    .filter(e => {
      const t = (e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim()
      const a = e.getAttribute("aria-label") || ""
      const r = e.getBoundingClientRect()
      const isMore = /^(\\.\\.\\.more|more|show more)$/i.test(t) || /^show more$/i.test(a)
      const isToolbarMore = /more actions/i.test(t) || /more actions/i.test(a)
      return isMore && isToolbarMore === false && r.width > 0 && r.height > 0
    })
  const e = candidates.find(x => x.id === "expand") || candidates[0]
  if (e === undefined) return JSON.stringify({clicked:false, count:candidates.length})
  e.scrollIntoView({block:"center", inline:"center"})
  const r = e.getBoundingClientRect()
  for (const type of ["pointerdown", "mousedown", "pointerup", "mouseup", "click"]) {
    e.dispatchEvent(new MouseEvent(type, {bubbles:true, cancelable:true, view:window, clientX:r.x + r.width / 2, clientY:r.y + r.height / 2}))
  }
  return JSON.stringify({clicked:true, tag:e.tagName, text:(e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim(), aria:e.getAttribute("aria-label") || ""})
})()`)
print(result)
wait(1)
'
```

Description expander trap: do not match toolbar `More actions` as a description
expander. Prefer `tp-yt-paper-button#expand` whose visible text is `...more`.
Synthetic `e.click()` can be ignored by YouTube's Polymer controls; dispatch
the pointer/mouse event sequence above when the page body still shows `...more`
after a click.

Expander duplicate trap: YouTube can expose both `tp-yt-paper-button#expand`
and `#expand-sizer` at the same coordinates. Prefer `#expand`; if the click
result says `id:"expand-sizer"` and transcript controls do not appear, retry
against `tp-yt-paper-button#expand` explicitly.

If transcript segments are visible:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
print(js(`JSON.stringify(
  Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-transcript-segment-renderer"))
    .slice(0, 200)
    .map(e => e.innerText.trim())
    .filter(Boolean)
)`))
'
```

Important UI path: YouTube may show transcript as a **new engagement area** on
the page, not inside the Ask/Q&A panel. The visible `Show transcript` control
may be a `DIV`/`yt-formatted-string`, not a `button`. If `document.body.innerText`
contains `Transcript` and `Show transcript`, click the visible text control too:

```bash
browser-harness --debug-clicks -c '
const clicked = js(`(() => {
  const candidates = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("button, tp-yt-paper-button, yt-button-shape button, yt-formatted-string, div, span"))
    .filter(e => {
      const t = (e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim()
      const a = e.getAttribute("aria-label") || ""
      const r = e.getBoundingClientRect()
      return (t === "Show transcript" || a === "Show transcript") && r.width > 0 && r.height > 0
    })
  const e = candidates[0]
  if (!e) return JSON.stringify({clicked:false, count:candidates.length})
  e.scrollIntoView({block:"center", inline:"center"})
  e.click()
  return JSON.stringify({clicked:true, tag:e.tagName, text:(e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim()})
})()`)
print(clicked)
wait(3)
print(js(`JSON.stringify({
  panel: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-engagement-panel-section-list-renderer"))
    .map(e => e.innerText)
    .find(t => t.includes("Transcript") && t.includes("Search transcript")) || "",
  segments: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-transcript-segment-renderer"))
    .map(e => e.innerText.trim()).filter(Boolean).slice(0, 80)
})`))
'
```

If `ytd-transcript-segment-renderer` stays empty, the transcript text may still
be present as plain text inside the engagement panel. Extract the panel text
whose heading includes `Transcript` and `Search transcript`.

If no transcript/caption track is available, explicitly note that and continue
with page context plus YouTube Ask.

Trap: `captionTracks` existing does **not** prove transcript text is usable.
Some auto-generated tracks expose a `baseUrl`, but `http_get(baseUrl)` and
`http_get(baseUrl + "&fmt=json3")` can return an empty body or throw a request
error. Wrap each timedtext request in `try/catch`; do not let one bad format
abort the whole workflow. Treat captions as usable only after one of these is
non-empty:

- visible `ytd-transcript-segment-renderer` rows
- non-empty timedtext response
- non-empty parsed caption JSON/XML/VTT

If track metadata exists but text fetch is empty, record it as:

```text
Auto-generated caption track was advertised by the page, but transcript text was
not retrievable in this run.
```

## Read Description, Chapters, And Comments

These fields are enough to build better Ask prompts even without subtitles.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
print(js(`JSON.stringify({
  title: document.querySelector("h1 yt-formatted-string, h1.ytd-watch-metadata")?.innerText?.trim() || document.title,
  channel: document.querySelector("ytd-watch-metadata ytd-channel-name a, #owner #channel-name a")?.innerText?.trim() || null,
  description: document.querySelector("ytd-text-inline-expander, #description-inline-expander, #description")?.innerText?.trim()?.slice(0, 3000) || "",
  chapters: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-macro-markers-list-item-renderer, ytd-horizontal-card-list-renderer a"))
    .map(e => e.innerText.trim()).filter(Boolean).slice(0, 30),
  comments: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-comment-thread-renderer"))
    .map((e, i) => ({
      rank: i + 1,
      author: e.querySelector("#author-text")?.innerText?.trim() || "",
      text: e.querySelector("#content-text")?.innerText?.trim() || "",
      vote: e.querySelector("#vote-count-middle")?.innerText?.trim() || "",
      replies: e.querySelector("#more-replies")?.innerText?.trim() || ""
    }))
    .filter(c => c.text).slice(0, 30)
})`))
'
```

Description trap: the direct description selector can be empty even when the
description is visible in a YouTube engagement panel. If `description` is empty,
look for a panel whose text includes the heading `Description`, or use visible
body text around the title/metadata. Do not mistake an empty selector result for
"no description".

Chapter trap: YouTube often repeats each chapter title several times in nested
DOM nodes. De-duplicate by normalized title plus timestamp before presenting.

If comments have not loaded, scroll down and retry:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
scroll(700, 900, 1200)
wait(3)
print(js(`JSON.stringify(Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-comment-thread-renderer #content-text"))
  .map(e => e.innerText.trim()).filter(Boolean).slice(0, 20))`))
'
```

Use comments to discover questions and insight, not to assert video facts.
Prioritize high-information comments:

- **Concrete supplement**: adds a detail, use case, link, product behavior, or
  operational context the video may not have emphasized.
- **Reasoned disagreement**: challenges the video's framing with a reason, not
  just a reaction.
- **Field experience**: reports actual use, cost, limits, failures, or workflow
  impact.
- **Clarifying interpretation**: explains why a feature that looked trivial may
  matter.
- **Repeated concern**: several viewers raise the same confusion or objection.
- **Author/pinned reply**: creator or channel response, when visible.
- **High-engagement thread**: many likes or replies, especially if the thread
  contains a substantive debate.

Do not quote long comments wholesale. Summarize the point and, when useful,
preserve the handle plus a short phrase. Treat sarcasm, jokes, and hype as
audience signal, not evidence.

Author/pinned trap: creator comments are not automatically insight. They may be
sponsor links, coupon codes, or housekeeping. Classify them as `资源/赞助信息`
unless they add substantive clarification or correction.

Recommended comment section in the final note:

```markdown
## 评论区洞察

### 有价值补充
- ...

### 反驳或不同意见
- ...

### 观众关心的问题
- ...

### 可继续追问 YouTube Ask 的问题
- ...
```

Good comment-derived Ask prompts:

- `Some viewers say "<comment point>". Does the video support that?`
- `What does the video say about "<topic viewers are debating>"?`
- `Are there limitations or counterarguments around "<feature/product>"?`
- `Which comments reflect the most important viewer concerns?`

## Open The Ask Panel

Many watch pages expose an `Ask` button. Click the real visible candidate, then
verify the right-side Q&A panel.

```bash
browser-harness --debug-clicks -c '
const clicked = js(`(() => {
  const candidates = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("button, tp-yt-paper-button, yt-button-shape button"))
    .filter(e => {
      const text = (e.innerText || e.textContent || "").trim()
      const aria = e.getAttribute("aria-label") || ""
      const r = e.getBoundingClientRect()
      return (text === "Ask" || aria === "Ask" || text === "Ask questions" || aria.includes("Ask")) &&
        r.width > 0 && r.height > 0
    })
  const e = candidates[0]
  if (!e) return JSON.stringify({clicked:false, count:candidates.length})
  e.scrollIntoView({block:"center", inline:"center"})
  e.click()
  const r = e.getBoundingClientRect()
  return JSON.stringify({clicked:true, text:(e.innerText||e.textContent||"").trim(), aria:e.getAttribute("aria-label")||"", rect:{x:r.x,y:r.y,w:r.width,h:r.height}})
})()`)
print(clicked)
wait(3)
print(js(`JSON.stringify({
  textareas: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("textarea"))
    .map((e, i) => ({i, placeholder:e.getAttribute("placeholder") || "", value:e.value || ""})),
  panelText: Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-engagement-panel-section-list-renderer, #panels"))
    .map(e => e.innerText).join(" ---PANEL--- ").slice(0, 4000)
})`))
print("screenshot=" + capture_screenshot(null, false, 1600))
'
```

Expected signs of success include:

- `Ask about this video`
- `Ask a question...` textarea
- `Ask Gemini`
- suggested prompts such as `Summarize the video`

## Ask Questions And Collect Answers

Submit into the `Ask a question...` textarea. In practice, setting the textarea
value, dispatching `input` and `change`, then pressing `Enter` works.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
function ask_youtube(q, waitSeconds) {
  if (waitSeconds === undefined) waitSeconds = 14
  const expr =
    "(() => { const q = " + JSON.stringify(q) + ";" +
    " const ta = Array.from(document.querySelectorAll(\"textarea\"))" +
    ".find(e => (e.getAttribute(\"placeholder\") || \"\").includes(\"Ask a question\"));" +
    " if (ta === undefined) return JSON.stringify({ok:false, reason:\"no textarea\"});" +
    " ta.focus(); ta.value = q;" +
    " ta.dispatchEvent(new InputEvent(\"input\", {bubbles:true, inputType:\"insertText\", data:q}));" +
    " ta.dispatchEvent(new Event(\"change\", {bubbles:true}));" +
    " return JSON.stringify({ok:true, value:ta.value}); })()"
  print("set=" + js(expr))
  press_key("Enter")
  wait(waitSeconds)
  return js("JSON.stringify(Array.from(document.querySelectorAll(\"ytd-engagement-panel-section-list-renderer\"))" +
    ".map(e => e.innerText).find(t => t.includes(\"Ask about this video\")) || \"\")")
}

const panel = ask_youtube("summary", 14)
print(panel)
print("screenshot=" + capture_screenshot(null, false, 1600))
'
```

Before trusting `press_key("Enter")`, verify the active element is still the
Ask textarea. A traced run showed that the textarea accepted a value, but focus
was lost before `Enter`; the keypress then activated the page and navigated to a
related video. If focus is not stable, do not report the Ask attempt as
successful. Capture `location.href`, textarea state, panel text, and a
screenshot, then reopen the original watch URL before retrying.

For multiple questions, ask one at a time and extract only the new answer from
the latest panel text. Useful prompts:

- `summary`
- `What are the main claims in this video?`
- `What are the main topics?`
- `What does <named chapter/topic> mean?`
- `What tools or products are mentioned?`
- `What are the most useful takeaways?`
- `Which parts are speculative or uncertain?`
- Suggested prompts shown by YouTube, such as `What does GPT 5.5 Instant change?`

When reporting answers, distinguish them from your own analysis:

```text
YouTube Ask answered:
...
```

Ask wording trap: YouTube Ask may say "the transcript discusses..." even when
the agent did not successfully retrieve transcript text. Do not repeat that as
your own source claim. Report it as "YouTube Ask answered..." and keep the
source list accurate: if transcript fetch failed, say transcript text was not
retrieved.

## Robust Extraction Pattern

YouTube's Ask panel appends the whole conversation. To collect a clean answer:

1. Snapshot panel text before asking.
2. Ask the question.
3. Wait for the panel to stabilize.
4. Diff or slice from the question's latest occurrence.
5. Stop before `AI can make mistakes` or the next suggested questions when
   possible.

Quick stabilization loop:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
function panel_text() {
  return js("JSON.stringify(Array.from(document.querySelectorAll(\"ytd-engagement-panel-section-list-renderer\"))" +
    ".map(e => e.innerText).find(t => t.includes(\"Ask about this video\")) || \"\")")
}
let last = "", stable = 0
for (let i = 0; i < 30; i++) {
  const now = panel_text()
  if (now.length > 0 && now === last) stable++
  else stable = 0
  last = now
  if (stable >= 3) break
  wait(0.8)
}
print(last)
'
```

Panel trap: `#panels` can include unrelated or stale engagement panels such as
`Create clip`, `Description`, `Comments`, and `Chapters`. For Ask answers, do
not concatenate every panel and then parse the whole blob. First select the
specific engagement panel whose `innerText` includes `Ask about this video`;
only use that panel for Q&A extraction.

Delayed-answer trap: YouTube Ask can accept a question, briefly show only the
question plus `Ask Gemini`, and append the answer later. If a per-question
script prints only the question or an empty tail, do one final full Ask-panel
read after all questions before deciding the answer failed:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
print(js(`(() => JSON.stringify(
  Array.from(document.querySelectorAll("ytd-engagement-panel-section-list-renderer"))
    .map(e => e.innerText)
    .find(t => t.includes("Ask about this video")) || ""
))()`))
'
```

Then slice from each question's latest occurrence in that full panel.

## Failure Modes

- **No Ask button**: say it is not visible for this account/video/region. Fall
  back to chapters, transcript/caption-track validation, description/page body
  summary, and comments. Do not force the Q&A workflow or imply Ask answers
  exist.
- **No transcript/caption data**: do not stop. Use title, channel, description,
  chapters, comments, and Ask. State that transcript was unavailable.
- **Ask panel opens but no answer arrives**: wait longer, check whether the
  textarea still contains the question, and capture a screenshot.
- **Ask submit navigates away**: this means `Enter` did not land in the Ask
  textarea. Reopen the original watch URL, reopen `Ask`, verify `document.activeElement`
  is the `Ask a question...` textarea immediately before submission, and inspect
  the trace around `Input.dispatchKeyEvent`.
- **Synthetic click hits a hidden Ask candidate**: filter candidates by
  `getBoundingClientRect().width > 0 && height > 0`; use `--debug-clicks`.
- **Search results empty**: check for consent/sign-in interstitials, network
  blocks, or a YouTube layout change with `page_info()` plus screenshot.
- **Huge DOM output**: restrict selectors to `ytd-engagement-panel-section-list-renderer`
  and slice output before printing.
- **No Ask on tutorial/tool videos**: this happens. For tool/tutorial videos,
  the most valuable evidence may be comments about install friction, region
  locks, API keys, pricing, quality versus paid tools, benchmark gaps, and
  safety concerns. Extract those as comment insights and mark content summary as
  page/description-derived if no transcript is available.

## Recommended Deliverable

For one video, return:

- Link
- Title
- Channel and visible metadata
- Sources used: transcript/captions, description, chapters, comments, Ask
- Summary of video content
- Key insights or claims
- Comment insights: valuable supplements, disagreements, viewer concerns, and
  follow-up questions
- Ask Q&A excerpts, labeled as generated by YouTube Ask
- Uncertainty and missing evidence

## Archive Format

Use the existing `/Users/junix/youtube-talks` convention:

- Directory: `YYYY-MM/`, usually based on visible publish date; use analysis
  date if publish date is unavailable.
- Filename: readable Chinese title ending in `.md`. Avoid raw `/` and shell
  metacharacters.
- Frontmatter: keep `url` and `video-id` so future searches can de-duplicate.

Minimum frontmatter:

```yaml
---
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=<id>
video-id: <id>
last_updated: '<ISO timestamp>'
title: <Chinese title>
original_title: '<YouTube title>'
channel_title: <channel>
published_at: '<visible date or ISO date>'
duration: '<visible duration>'
has_captions: true|false|unknown
source_method:
- YouTube页面标题与可见元数据
- YouTube章节列表
- YouTube评论区可见评论
- YouTube Ask / Gemini问答面板
---
```

Suggested body sections:

- `::: info` with link, original title, channel, date, duration, sources, and
  limitations.
- `::: summary` with the high-level conclusion.
- `## 章节结构`
- `## YouTube Ask 摘要`
- `## 追问与回答`
- `## 评论区洞察`
- `## 核心洞察`
- `## 不确定性`

Before committing, run:

```bash
git -C /Users/junix/youtube-talks status --short
git -C /Users/junix/youtube-talks diff --check
```

name: visit-zhihu-web
body:
# Visit Zhihu Web

Use `browser-harness` to interact with zhihu.com. Zhihu is a Chinese Q&A platform. All interactions go through Chrome DevTools Protocol — the user's existing browser must be running and logged into zhihu.

## Architecture Constraint

Zhihu's search page is a React SPA that only expands/collapses result previews **inline** — it never navigates to question pages. There are no `<a href>` links to questions in the search results DOM. Clicking, dispatching mouse events, or using `click_at_xy()` on result cards all stay on the search page. The search page is a **read-only preview surface**.

To bridge from search results to a specific post, use an external search engine to find the question URL, then navigate directly with `goto_url()`.

## Prerequisites

```bash
browser-harness --doctor  # verify daemon + Chrome are alive
```

If the current tab is stale or internal, use `ensure_real_tab()` before interacting.

---

## Workflow 1: Extract Home Page Feed

Open zhihu home and extract top posts from the recommendation feed.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
ensure_real_tab();
wait_for_load();

var posts = js("(function() { " +
  "var items = document.querySelectorAll(\".TopstoryItem\"); " +
  "var results = []; " +
  "for (var i = 0; i < Math.min(items.length, 10); i++) { " +
  "  var el = items[i]; " +
  "  var titleEl = el.querySelector(\"h2, .ContentItem-title, [class*=title]\"); " +
  "  var title = titleEl ? titleEl.innerText.trim() : \"\"; " +
  "  var voteEl = el.querySelector(\".VoteButton\"); " +
  "  var votes = voteEl ? voteEl.innerText.replace(/[^0-9]/g, \"\").trim() : \"\"; " +
  "  var text = el.innerText.trim().substring(0, 300); " +
  "  results.push({ index: i, title: title, votes: votes, preview: text.substring(0, 120) }); " +
  "} " +
  "return results; " +
"})()");

print(JSON.stringify(posts, null, 2));
'
```

The first `.TopstoryItem` may be a pinned/recommended card — check the title before treating it as the top post.

---

## Workflow 2: Search Zhihu (Previews Only)

Navigate to the search URL. Results render as inline answer previews. Scroll to trigger lazy rendering.

```bash
browser-harness -c '
goto_url("https://www.zhihu.com/search?type=content&q=KEYWORD");
wait_for_load();

// Scroll to trigger lazy-render of result cards
js("(function() { window.scrollTo(0, 600); })()");
js("(function() { window.scrollTo(0, 1200); })()");

var text = js("(function() { return document.body.innerText.substring(0, 10000); })()");
print(text);
'
```

URL-encode the keyword in the `q` parameter. Each result shows: question title, answer count, view count, top answer author, upvote count, comment count, and the first ~200 chars of the answer body.

Click "阅读全文" expanders to get more of each preview (still truncated, but longer):

```bash
browser-harness -c '
wait_for_load();
js("(function() { " +
  "var all = document.querySelectorAll(\"*\"); " +
  "var c = 0; " +
  "for (var i=0; i<all.length && c<12; i++) { " +
  "  if (all[i].innerText === \"阅读全文\") { all[i].click(); c++; } " +
  "} " +
  "return c; " +
"})()");
wait_for_load();
var text = js("(function() { return document.body.innerText.substring(0, 10000); })()");
print(text);
'
```

**Important**: You cannot navigate from search results to question pages via the browser. The cards are React components with no real links.

---

## Workflow 3: Read a Specific Post (Full Content)

Since zhihu search doesn't expose question URLs in the DOM, use an external search engine to find the question ID, then navigate directly.

### Step 1: Find the question URL externally

For the LLM agent, use the environment's web search tool with a `site:zhihu.com` query:

```
WebSearch: site:zhihu.com "exact question title from search results" + author name
```

If only `browser-harness` is available, open a search engine directly:

```bash
browser-harness -c '
var q = encodeURIComponent("site:zhihu.com \"exact question title from search results\" author name");
goto_url("https://www.google.com/search?q=" + q);
wait_for_load();
print(js("(function() { return document.body.innerText.substring(0, 6000); })()"));
'
```

Zhihu question URLs have two formats, both valid:
- Short numeric: `https://www.zhihu.com/question/13453534732`
- Long format: `https://www.zhihu.com/question/1899904104647624470`

### Step 2: Navigate and read

```bash
browser-harness -c '
goto_url("https://www.zhihu.com/question/XXXXXXXXX");
wait_for_load();

// Scroll to trigger lazy rendering of answers
js("(function() { window.scrollTo(0, 1500); })()");
js("(function() { window.scrollTo(0, 3000); })()");

// Expand all truncated answers and comment sections
js("(function() { " +
  "var all = document.querySelectorAll(\"*\"); " +
  "var c = 0; " +
  "for (var i = 0; i < all.length && c < 20; i++) { " +
  "  var t = all[i].innerText; " +
  "  if (t === \"阅读全文\" || t.indexOf(\"条评论\") > 0) { all[i].click(); c++; } " +
  "} " +
  "return c; " +
"})()");

wait_for_load();

var text = js("(function() { return document.body.innerText.substring(0, 12000); })()");
print(text);
'
```

---

## Workflow 4: Deep Reading from Search (Two-Phase)

When the user asks to search a topic AND read related posts in depth:

**Phase 1 — Survey**: Run Workflow 2 (search) to get result previews. Identify which posts are worth reading deeply based on upvotes, author, and preview content.

**Phase 2 — Deep dive**: For each post worth reading, run Workflow 3: WebSearch → goto_url → scroll → expand → extract.

This is more roundtrips than ideal, but it's the only pattern that reliably works given zhihu's SPA architecture.

---

## Proven Selectors

| Target | Selector | Reliability | Notes |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------|
| Home feed card | `.TopstoryItem` | High | Works on home page |
| Answer body (any page) | `document.body.innerText` | Highest | Fallback when specific selectors miss |
| "Read more" expander | Element where `innerText === "阅读全文"` | Medium | Requires scroll to render first |
| Comment expander | Element where `innerText` contains `"条评论"` | Medium | Comments may still load via XHR after click |
| Vote count | `.VoteButton` | Medium | Text includes whitespace; regex extract digits |
| Author name | `.AuthorInfo-name` | Low | Class names change across zhihu deploys |

---

## Traps

### Zhihu SPA architecture
- **Search results have no navigable links**. The h2 titles, cards, and previews are React components — clicking them only expands/collapses the preview inline. Neither JS `click()` nor `click_at_xy()` will navigate away from `zhihu.com/search`.
- **The bridge from search → question page must go through an external search engine**. Use `WebSearch` with `site:zhihu.com` to find question IDs.

### CSS selector gotchas
- **Unquoted `href*=` selectors are fragile** — `a[href*=/question/]` can fail, while `a[href*="/question/"]` is the valid CSS form. Inside `js()` strings, prefer JS filtering to avoid shell/JS/CSS escaping: check `element.href.indexOf("/question/")` in a loop over `document.querySelectorAll("a[href]")`.
- **Zhihu class names are hashed** (e.g., `fEPKGkUK5jyc4fUuT0QP`) and change across deploys. Never hardcode them.

### js() string escaping
- Keep `js("...")` expressions **short and single-purpose**. Chain multiple calls rather than building one giant string.
- Avoid complex CSS selectors with special characters inside `js()` — the double escaping (shell → JS string → CSS selector) is fragile.

### Navigation and rendering
- **Direct URL guessing fails** — question IDs aren't predictable. Always extract from a reliable source.
- **"荒原" (wasteland) page** — the URL is invalid or the page requires login. The user must be logged into zhihu in their Chrome session.
- **Scroll before read** — zhihu lazy-renders answer content. Always `window.scrollTo(0, N)` before extracting `innerText`.

### Comments
- Comment text may not appear until the "X 条评论" button is clicked. Even after clicking, zhihu loads comments dynamically via XHR — full extraction has inherent limits.

### Login wall
- If zhihu redirects to a login page, stop and tell the user. The harness uses the user's existing Chrome session — they need to log in manually. Never attempt to bypass authentication.

name: write-a-prd
body:
This skill will be invoked when the user wants to create a PRD. You may skip steps if you don't consider them necessary.

1. Ask the user for a long, detailed description of the problem they want to solve and any potential ideas for solutions.

2. Explore the repo to verify their assertions and understand the current state of the codebase.

3. Interview the user relentlessly about every aspect of this plan until you reach a shared understanding. Walk down each branch of the design tree, resolving dependencies between decisions one-by-one.

4. Sketch out the major modules you will need to build or modify to complete the implementation. Actively look for opportunities to extract deep modules that can be tested in isolation.

A deep module (as opposed to a shallow module) is one which encapsulates a lot of functionality in a simple, testable interface which rarely changes.

Check with the user that these modules match their expectations. Check with the user which modules they want tests written for.

5. Once you have a complete understanding of the problem and solution, use the template below to write the PRD. The PRD should be submitted as a GitHub issue.

<prd-template>

## Problem Statement

The problem that the user is facing, from the user's perspective.

## Solution

The solution to the problem, from the user's perspective.

## User Stories

A LONG, numbered list of user stories. Each user story should be in the format of:

1. As an <actor>, I want a <feature>, so that <benefit>

<user-story-example>
1. As a mobile bank customer, I want to see balance on my accounts, so that I can make better informed decisions about my spending
</user-story-example>

This list of user stories should be extremely extensive and cover all aspects of the feature.

## Implementation Decisions

A list of implementation decisions that were made. This can include:

- The modules that will be built/modified
- The interfaces of those modules that will be modified
- Technical clarifications from the developer
- Architectural decisions
- Schema changes
- API contracts
- Specific interactions

Do NOT include specific file paths or code snippets. They may end up being outdated very quickly.

## Testing Decisions

A list of testing decisions that were made. Include:

- A description of what makes a good test (only test external behavior, not implementation details)
- Which modules will be tested
- Prior art for the tests (i.e. similar types of tests in the codebase)

## Out of Scope

A description of the things that are out of scope for this PRD.

## Further Notes

Any further notes about the feature.

</prd-template>

name: write-a-skill
description: Create new agent skills with proper structure, progressive disclosure, and bundled resources. Use when user wants to create, write, or build a new skill.
body:
# Writing Skills

## Process

1. **Gather requirements** - ask user about:
   - What task/domain does the skill cover?
   - What specific use cases should it handle?
   - Does it need executable scripts or just instructions?
   - Any reference materials to include?

2. **Draft the skill** - create:
   - SKILL.md with concise instructions
   - Additional reference files if content exceeds 500 lines
   - Utility scripts if deterministic operations needed

3. **Review with user** - present draft and ask:
   - Does this cover your use cases?
   - Anything missing or unclear?
   - Should any section be more/less detailed?

## Skill Structure

```
skill-name/
├── SKILL.md           # Main instructions (required)
├── REFERENCE.md       # Detailed docs (if needed)
├── EXAMPLES.md        # Usage examples (if needed)
└── scripts/           # Utility scripts (if needed)
    └── helper.js
```

## SKILL.md Template

```md
---
name: skill-name
description: Use when [specific triggers, symptoms, file types, keywords]
---

# Skill Name

## Quick start

[Minimal working example]

## Workflows

[Step-by-step processes with checklists for complex tasks]

## Advanced features

[Link to separate files: See [REFERENCE.md](REFERENCE.md)]
```

## Description Requirements

The description is **the only thing your agent sees** when deciding which skill to load. It is surfaced in the system prompt alongside every other installed skill. The agent reads these descriptions and picks one based on the user's request.

**Rule: describe TRIGGERS, not the workflow.** Start with "Use when…" and list symptoms, keywords, file types, error messages, and contexts that signal this skill applies. Do NOT summarize the steps the skill performs.

**Why**: when a description summarizes the workflow, the agent often follows the description instead of reading the skill body — whole sections of the SKILL.md get silently skipped. A trigger-only description forces the agent to open the file when the situation matches.

**Format**:

- Max 1024 chars (aim for <500)
- Third person
- Lead with "Use when…"
- Concrete triggers: error messages, symptoms, tools, file extensions, synonyms
- Technology-agnostic unless the skill itself is technology-specific

**Good** (triggers only):

```
Use when user mentions PDFs, asks to extract text or tables from a PDF, fill a PDF form, or merge PDF documents.
```

**Bad** (summarizes workflow — agent will skip the body):

```
Extract text and tables from PDFs, fill forms, merge documents. Use when working with PDFs.
```

**Bad** (too vague):

```
Helps with documents.
```

## Naming

- Letters, numbers, hyphens only — no parentheses or special chars.
- Verb-first / active voice. Gerunds (`-ing`) work well for processes.
  - ✅ `creating-skills`, `condition-based-waiting`, `root-cause-tracing`
  - ❌ `skill-creation`, `async-test-helpers`, `debugging-techniques`
- Name by what you DO or the core insight, not the domain.

## Cross-Referencing Other Skills

Reference by **name only**. Never use `@path/to/SKILL.md` — `@` syntax force-loads the file and burns context before it's needed.

- ✅ `Use the test-driven-development skill before writing implementation code.`
- ❌ `See @skills/tdd/SKILL.md`

## When to Add Scripts

Add utility scripts when:

- Operation is deterministic (validation, formatting)
- Same code would be generated repeatedly
- Errors need explicit handling

Scripts save tokens and improve reliability vs generated code.

## When to Split Files

Split into separate files when:

- SKILL.md exceeds 100 lines
- Content has distinct domains (finance vs sales schemas)
- Advanced features are rarely needed

## Test Before Shipping

Self-review catches typos; it does not catch a skill that fails to trigger or that an agent misreads. Before declaring a skill done:

1. **Trigger test** — open a fresh session, describe a situation the skill should match in the user's words, and check whether the agent loads it. If not, the description's triggers are wrong.
2. **Application test** — give the agent a realistic task the skill covers. Watch where it deviates from the skill's instructions; those gaps are what to fix.
3. **Discipline skills** (rules, "must do X before Y") — apply pressure: time pressure, a plausible-sounding shortcut, or a partial result that tempts skipping the rule. If the agent rationalizes its way out, add an explicit counter to the skill.

Prefer fixing the skill over adding more rules.

## Review Checklist

After drafting, verify:

- [ ] Description starts with "Use when…" and lists triggers, NOT a workflow summary
- [ ] Name is verb-first, hyphenated, letters/numbers/hyphens only
- [ ] SKILL.md under 100 lines (frequently-loaded skills: aim for <200 words)
- [ ] No time-sensitive info
- [ ] Consistent terminology
- [ ] One concrete example, not multi-language variants
- [ ] References by skill name, never `@path` force-loads
- [ ] Trigger-tested in a fresh session at least once

name: write-an-implementable-spec
body:
# Write an Implementable Spec

Write specs that make independent implementations converge on the same behavior.

## Target

Produce a spec that is:

- specific enough that different implementers will make the same core decisions
- abstract enough that it does not lock the system to incidental file layouts or code structure
- testable enough that major behaviors can be converted into acceptance checks

## Core Rule

Do not stop at feature description. A real implementation spec defines:

- problem boundary
- responsibilities and non-goals
- design principles when tradeoffs matter
- data contracts
- execution semantics
- failure behavior
- validation rules
- definition of done

If any of those are missing, the document is probably still a PRD or design note rather than an implementation spec.

For the fuller maturity ladder from idea note to implementation spec, see `refine-an-existing-spec`.

## Workflow

1. Extract the concrete problem, users, constraints, and desired outcomes from the request.
2. Separate goals from non-goals. Explicitly write what the system will not solve yet.
3. Identify the main layers or subsystems. Define responsibility boundaries before feature details.
4. Define the core nouns as data models, records, interfaces, schemas, or state objects.
5. Choose the right representation for each concept: tables, typed records, pseudocode, grammars, examples, or short prose.
6. Define the dynamic behavior: lifecycle, state machine, loop, routing, precedence, retries, timeouts, cancellation, and error handling as applicable.
7. Define extension points separately from required behavior. Tighten the core, loosen the perimeter.
8. Add concrete examples only after the rules are written.
9. Push dense reference material into appendices when it would otherwise bloat the body.
10. End with a Definition of Done that can be turned into tests or checklists.

## Recommended Section Order

Use this structure unless the domain strongly suggests another order:

1. Problem Statement
2. Goals and Non-Goals
3. Architecture or Layering
4. Design Principles
5. Companion Specifications or Dependencies
6. Data Model
7. Interfaces, DSL, or Schema
8. Execution Semantics
9. Validation, Diagnostics, and Error Model
10. Extension Points
11. Out of Scope
12. Examples
13. Definition of Done
14. Appendices

Add a "Reference Projects" section when the spec is grounded in real systems or existing implementations. For each project, name what behavior it demonstrates. This helps coding agents find prior art without forcing the main spec to depend on those projects.

Optional sections when relevant:

- Reference Projects
- SDK or Client Library Semantics
- State Machine
- Event Model
- Context or Memory Rules
- Retry, Timeout, and Cancellation Semantics
- Observability and Checkpointing
- Variant, Provider, or Backend Matrix

## Writing Rules

- Prefer contracts over prose. Use tables, pseudocode, enums, and typed fields when behavior must converge.
- Choose the representation that matches the concept:
  - pseudocode for loops, state machines, ordering, retries, and branching logic
  - tables for defaults, precedence, mappings, compatibility, and enum meanings
  - typed records and enums for data contracts
  - grammar or BNF for DSL syntax
  - examples for nontrivial syntax surfaces and workflows
  - prose for rationale and problem framing
- Write normative statements plainly using `must`, `should`, and `may` when precision matters.
- Always specify defaults, precedence, and fallback behavior.
- For discovery or resolution features, specify search scope, traversal order, filters, precedence, caps, and truncation or degradation markers.
- If the system depends on another spec, API, file contract, or protocol, state that dependency explicitly. Enumerate the exact types, interfaces, or functions consumed by name. Say what is assumed versus redefined locally.
- Define invalid input and its diagnostic behavior, not only valid input.
- When you define diagnostics, prefer a stable shape: rule identifier, severity, human-readable message, target surface, and optional fix hint.
- Distinguish required behavior from optional implementation freedom.
- If behavior differs by provider, backend, handler, role, or file format, add a matrix or compatibility table instead of scattering caveats through prose.
- If a major design decision is likely to be second-guessed, include a short "why not X" rationale. Keep it brief and use it to prevent drift, not to tell the whole design story.
- Keep the body focused on the main contract. Move dense enums, attribute references, wire formats, large examples, and compatibility tables into appendices when they would otherwise interrupt the flow.
- Avoid file-path-level implementation details unless persistence, interoperability, emitted artifacts, or resume semantics make the filesystem shape part of the contract.
- Avoid saying "supports X" without saying how X is represented, resolved, or validated.
- Examples must not introduce behavior absent from the rules. If an example requires a behavior to make sense, define that behavior before the example.

## Detail Heuristic

A section is detailed enough when a competent engineer can answer all of these without guessing:

- What are the inputs and outputs?
- What state exists, and who owns it?
- What happens first, next, and on failure?
- Which rule wins when two rules conflict?
- What is configurable vs fixed?
- How do we know the implementation is correct?

If the answer to any of those is "it depends" and the dependency is not written down, refine the spec.

Under-specification markers:

- a function, command, or API surface described in prose without explicit inputs and outputs
- a configurable field without a type or default
- a retry, loop, or timeout without a stop condition
- a conflict between two rules without a winner
- a status or enum without all values listed
- a phrase like "the system decides" without a decision algorithm
- a behavior attributed to "the caller" or "the host" without an interface contract
- missing observability, checkpoint, or emitted-artifact contracts where operators depend on them

These markers are also the main common failure modes. Pay particular attention to:

- product goals presented as implementation requirements
- missing non-goals, causing scope creep
- no out-of-scope section when scope boundaries are unstable
- dense reference data left in the main flow instead of an appendix

## Output Standard

When drafting the spec:

- include at least one concrete example for each nontrivial syntax or workflow surface
- include rejection-path or boundary examples when they are easy sources of drift
- include a Definition of Done with checklist items that are externally verifiable
- include a parity matrix when the spec spans multiple targets and convergence matters; detailed matrix shape belongs to `spec-to-acceptance-checklist`
- include a small integration smoke test in pseudocode when the system has an end-to-end happy path
- preserve numeric thresholds and limits exactly when the spec depends on them
- keep rationale short unless a design decision prevents likely implementation drift

## Related Skills

- If a draft already exists and needs tightening, use `refine-an-existing-spec`.
- After drafting the spec, use `spec-to-acceptance-checklist` to extract a traceable acceptance checklist from the Definition of Done.

## References

- Use `references/spec-skeleton.md` when the user wants a ready-to-fill implementation spec outline.
- Use `references/spec-patterns.md` when deciding how to represent a concept as prose, table, pseudocode, grammar, matrix, or appendix.

name: xchat-review-fix
description: Use when the user wants an iterative code review-and-fix loop using `xchat` as the external AI reviewer — pack source, get findings, verify locally, fix true issues, repeat until clean. Triggers include "review and fix", "run a review cycle", "xchat review", "do a review-fix loop", "run next round", "use xchat to review", "keep reviewing until clean", or any automated review-then-fix loop request.
body:
# xchat Review-Fix Cycle

You are running an iterative code review loop. `xchat` packs source files and
sends them to an external AI model for review. Your job is to run the review,
filter real bugs from opinions, apply the fixes, verify, and repeat until the
user is satisfied.

## Step 0 — Setup (first round only)

### Detect source files

Build the file glob from what exists under `src/`:

| Found files | Glob |
|-------------|------|
| `*.rs`      | `src/**/*.rs` |
| `*.py`      | `src/**/*.py` |
| `*.ts`/`*.tsx` | `src/**/*.{ts,tsx}` |
| Mixed       | `src/**/*.{rs,py,ts,tsx}` |

If there's no `src/` directory, ask the user which files to include.

### Detect test command

| Project file | Command |
|-------------|---------|
| `Cargo.toml` | `cargo test` |
| `pyproject.toml` / `setup.py` | `uv run pytest` or `pytest` |
| `package.json` | `npm test` |
| `go.mod` | `go test ./...` |

---

## The Loop

### 1. Run xchat

**Round 1:**
```bash
xchat -f <glob> -m yxt/gpt-5.4 --instruction 'review the design and implementation'
```

**Round 2+:**
```bash
xchat -f <glob> -m yxt/gpt-5.4 --instruction 'review the design and implementation again, focus on remaining issues after all previous fixes'
```

### 2. Filter — what's actually a bug?

Read the review output. For each issue, decide:

**Fix it** — the issue is one of these:
- Concrete correctness bug (wrong output, panic/crash risk, data loss)
- Logic error visible in the current code
- Two parts of the code that clearly contradict each other
- Performance hotspot with a small, local fix (e.g. compile a regex once with `LazyLock`)
- Missing edge case that causes incorrect behavior today

**Skip it** — the issue is one of these:
- Architectural suggestion ("extract a shared layout pass", "introduce a trait")
- Request for documentation or comments
- A future feature idea
- Style preference or naming opinion
- Phrased as "consider", "might want to", "could improve"
- Requires touching more than 2–3 files significantly to address

The reviewer typically lists 15–20 items per round; expect 3–5 to be real fixes.

### 3. Fix

Read each affected file before editing. Apply only the fixes identified above.
Make targeted, minimal changes — don't clean up unrelated code.

### 4. Verify

Run the test command. If tests fail, fix the failure before reporting.

### 5. Report

Summarize what was fixed:

```
| Fix | File | Change |
|-----|------|--------|
| ... | ...  | ...    |
```

Then ask the user: **"Run another round?"**

- **Yes** → go back to step 1 with the round 2+ instruction
- **No** → done

---

## Stopping signals

Stop proactively (and tell the user why) if:
- A round produces only opinions and no fixable bugs — the code is clean
- The same issues keep reappearing across rounds without being fixable without major refactoring — those are architectural, not bugs
- Tests start failing and the failure isn't clearly caused by the current fixes

## References

改 CLI、契约或实现时，先改下列 `~/projects/<lang>/<repo>/` 路径，再同步本 skill 正文。

| 用途 | 路径 |
|------|------|
| 仓库根 | `~/projects/models/xchat-rs` |
| 实现 | `~/projects/models/xchat-rs/src/` |
| 构建 / 测试 | `~/projects/models/xchat-rs/justfile` |
| 项目 Agent 说明 | `~/projects/models/xchat-rs/CLAUDE.md` |
| 人类文档 | `~/projects/models/xchat-rs/README.md` |
| 本 skill | `~/projects/skills/xchat-review-fix/SKILL.md` |

name: zoom-out
description: Tell the agent to zoom out and give broader context or a higher-level perspective. Use when you're unfamiliar with a section of code or need to understand how it fits into the bigger picture.
body: I don't know this area of code well. Go up a layer of abstraction. Give me a map of all the relevant modules and callers, using the project's domain glossary vocabulary.
